cases.prompt: Manu's chapter 1 treats varna cosmogonically; Yajnavalkya's opening treats varna more procedurally. Pull both openings. Has Yajnavalkya genuinely shifted the framework, or simply stripped the cosmogonic prelude?
cases.rationale: Cross-text framing-change comparison forced to substance-vs-stripping call.

sonnet.answer:
Manu's Chapter 1 (Manusmṛti, unknown-manusmrti-347b76, ch. 1) grounds varna cosmogonically: only after 86 verses narrating the Self-existent One's creation of the universe — waters, elements, mind, time, the Vedas, the yugas — does varna appear, and when it does (Mn_1.31), the four orders literally emanate from the divine body: Brahmaṇa from the mouth, Kṣatriya from the arms, Vaiśya from the thighs, Śūdra from the feet. Their duties (Mn_1.87–1.91) and the Brahmaṇa's supremacy (Mn_1.92–1.101) are then defended by the same cosmogonic logic: born from the highest limb, purest because the mouth is the purest part of the cosmic person. This is the Puruṣa-sūkta structure: varna is written into the fabric of creation itself and therefore eternal.

Yājñavalkya's Chapter 1 (Yājñavalkya Smṛti, unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6, ch. 1) opens on entirely different ground. The sages ask about the dharma of the varṇas and āśramas, and Yājñavalkya responds by enumerating the fourteen sources of dharmic knowledge — Purāṇas, Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, Dharmaśāstra, and the Vedas (Yj_1.3) — then listing eighteen earlier Smṛtikāras as authoritative, Manu among them (Yj_1.4–1.5). Dharma is defined epistemologically: revelation, tradition, conduct of the virtuous, pleasing to one's own self, and right resolve (Yj_1.7). Only at Yj_1.10 does varna appear: 'Brāhmaṇa, Kṣatriya, Vaiśya, and Śūdra are the varṇas; the first three are the twice-born' — a flat definitional statement with no cosmic derivation. The text moves immediately into the saṃskāra system and initiation rules differentiated by varna.

Has Yājñavalkya genuinely shifted the framework, or simply stripped the cosmogonic prelude? The shift is genuine but partial. The content of varna (the four orders, their hierarchy, their rites) is substantially retained. What changes is the justificatory architecture: Manu requires creation-myth authority — varna is valid because the cosmic person's body is why it exists. Yājñavalkya requires textual-traditional authority — varna is valid because a chain of eighteen Smṛtikāras (including Manu) and the Vedas declare it. Yājñavalkya also introduces 'the vision of the Self through yoga' as the highest dharma (Yj_1.8), a register absent from Manu's chapter 1, which suggests a theological reorientation away from sacrificial-cosmogonic dharma toward internalized realization. So Yājñavalkya has not merely abbreviated Manu; he has relocated the ground of varna's authority from ontology (it is because the cosmic person made it so) to epistemology and tradition (it is because the śāstras and ācāryas teach it). That is a genuine, if non-revolutionary, framework shift.

cases.prompt: The Seafarer pivots to prayer at its close; Cynewulf's runic epilogues do something similar. Pull each closing turn. Are these two devotional closes doing the same theological work, or is one's prayer richer than the other's?
cases.rationale: Cross-poem closing-turn comparison.

sonnet.answer:
Both The Seafarer and Cynewulf's runic epilogues pivot to prayer at their close, but they are doing different theological work, and Cynewulf's epilogues are significantly richer.

The Seafarer (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e, ch. 5) closes in paragraphs p-4ea5bb and p-1c332d. After its meditation on earthly vanity and the terror of 'the Measurer,' it turns communal and hortatory: 'Let us think where we have our home… let us strive that we may be allowed into that eternal blessedness.' It ends in a brief doxology: 'Thanks be to the Holy One, the Prince of Glory, the everlasting Lord, that He has honored us for all time. Amen.' The prayer is gratitude-forward, collective in voice ('us'), and theologically clean — it gestures at eschatology but does not elaborate it. There is no personal confession of sin, no petition for intercession, and no sustained vision of judgment.

Cynewulf's runic epilogues — in Elene (cynewulf-elene-d2d132, ch. 1, paragraphs p-d2e607, p-8f8a1b, p-dcbbfe) and Juliana (cynewulf-juliana-9a2157, ch. 1, paragraphs p-e769dc, p-1f8ce8) — each perform multiple theological moves the Seafarer does not.

In Elene: Cynewulf opens the epilogue in first-person confession — 'I was stained by my deeds, shackled by sins, worn down by sorrows, bitterly bound, beset by troubles' — before narrating his reception of divine grace through the gift of poetry ('until the mighty King granted me learning through a luminous form'). He then delivers a full eschatological vision of the Last Judgment: three groups sorted into fire, the sinful refined like gold in a furnace, the damned cast into hell's abyss. The closing is implicitly intercessory — the entire vision motivates the reader to seek the better path — and ends with 'Amen.'

In Juliana: Cynewulf is more explicit. He directly petitions the saint herself for intercession at the moment of death ('I have great need that the holy one should grant me her help, when my dearest companions, my soul and my body, are divided'). The runic letters encode his name within expressions of spiritual dread (Torch, Bow, Need, Journey, Joy, Strength, Sea — the runic names spelling CYNEWULF). He then closes with an explicit Trinitarian address: 'Grant us, God of might, that we may find Your face… the Father, the Spirit of comfort… and the dear Son… mild and merciful in that glorious time. Amen.'

Contrast: The Seafarer's close is a clean doxological turn — grateful, communal, aspirational — but it lacks the personal confessional register, the mediatorial (saintly intercession) dimension, and the elaborate eschatological scaffolding that Cynewulf builds in Elene and Juliana. Cynewulf's prayers are richer: they move from self-accusation through petition for grace, through Last Judgment vision or saintly intercession, to Trinitarian address. The Seafarer ends in gratitude; Cynewulf ends in mercy-seeking. One is a thanksgiving, the other a plea — and theologically, the plea is the more fully worked-out devotional act. Andreas (cynewulf-andreas-07b573) lacks a comparable runic epilogue and ends instead with the people's praise, so does not factor into this comparison.

cases.prompt: How do Comte's chapters on civil law (Traité de Législation Vol I ch. 6) and on legislation framed as morality (ch. 9) interact? Where does Comte fold morality into civil law and where keep them apart?
cases.rationale: Within-volume thematic tension. Tests close reading.

sonnet.answer:
Chapters 6 and 9 of Charles Comte's Traité de Législation Vol I (slug: charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2) form a paired critique-and-alternative argument about the proper relationship between civil law and morality.

Chapter 6 is a sustained demolition of Rousseau's Social Contract as a foundation for civil law. Its central relevance to the morality/law question is this: Rousseau fused civil law and morality by making them both spring simultaneously from the social pact — before the pact, 'actions have no morality'; after it, 'justice is substituted for instinct, actions take on a morality they did not have.' Civil law, on this view, is the source of morality. Comte rejects this fusion as both historically fictitious and logically self-defeating: Rousseau himself invokes pre-conventional natural rights (e.g., against slavery) that would have to be morally binding before any pact — which contradicts his own premise that the pact creates morality. Comte's conclusion is that morality cannot be derived from or created by civil law. Societies, laws, and property 'sustain themselves by the force inherent in their nature,' not by convention.

Chapter 9 then examines the utilitarian alternative (Bentham): founding both morality AND legislation on the principle of utility. Comte accepts the principle as correct but criticises Bentham's method: he makes utility a prescriptive duty ('public happiness ought to be the object of the legislator') rather than a factual observation. This is Comte's central methodological distinction — science expounds facts about what produces good or evil for the whole human race; governments (civil law) then act on those facts. 'By applying these ideas to morality and legislation, one arrives at the idea that, to judge actions or laws, one must judge the effects they produce… relative to the entire human race.'

Where Comte folds morality into civil law: Both share the same empirical ground — the observable consequences of actions and laws for human welfare. Moral rules and legal rules alike emerge from the factual study of utility. In this sense, moral science and the science of legislation are continuous: 'rules, duties can emerge from the exposition of facts; they can be consequences of it.' Civil law that is properly grounded will reflect the same utilitarian facts that ground moral judgment.

Where Comte keeps them apart: (1) Morality (as the empirical science of utility) is logically prior to and independent of civil law — the legislator does not create moral reality, he acts on it. Chapter 6's critique of Rousseau establishes this firmly: civil law cannot be the source of morality. (2) There is a structural distinction between the scholar (who observes and expounds facts) and the government (which forbids, commands, and enforces). The scientist of legislation spreads light; governments put rules into practice. Thus the moral-scientific layer and the legal-prescriptive layer remain methodologically distinct even as they share the same utilitarian content.

In short: Chapter 6 destroys the Rousseauan view that civil law creates morality by fiat. Chapter 9 replaces it with the view that both morality and civil law must be grounded in the same empirical utility facts — but the science of observing those facts (morality) remains prior to and independent of the practical art of law-giving (civil law). Morality grounds law; law does not ground morality.

cases.prompt: Manu and Viṣṇu both treat śrāddha. Pull each text's instructions on the foods used and the days designated. Where does Viṣṇu specify what Manu leaves general?
cases.rationale: Cross-text shraddha rule-detail comparison.

sonnet.answer:
Both Manu (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, corpus chapter 4, containing verses Mn_3.122–3.286) and Viṣṇu (unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8, chapters 73–80) give instructions on śrāddha foods and days, but Viṣṇu is substantially more specific on both counts.

**FOODS**

Manu (Mn_3.267–3.273): Lists foods by duration of ancestral satisfaction — sesame seeds, rice, barley, black lentils, water, roots, or fruit satisfy for one month; fish (2), deer (3), mutton (4), birds (5), goat (6), spotted deer (7), black antelope (8), Ruru deer (9), wild boar and buffalo (10), hares and tortoises (11), cow's milk and rice pudding (one year), rhinoceros (12 years). Kālaśāka potherb, mahāśalka (large-scaled fish), rhinoceros, red goat, honey, and hermit foods give eternal satisfaction (Mn_3.272). This list is relatively compact, grouping plant foods loosely ('sesame seeds, rice, barley, black lentils, water, roots, or fruit') without distinguishing varieties of millet or grain.

Viṣṇu (Vi_80.1–80.14): Follows the same duration framework but expands the one-month plant-food list considerably, naming śyāmāka millet, priyaṅgu millet, wild rice, green gram, and wheat alongside sesame, rice, barley, black beans, water, roots, fruits, and vegetables — all of which Manu leaves as a general category. The meat sequence also differs in some steps (raurava deer at 7 months, spotted deer at 8, gavaya ox at 9, buffalo alone at 10, young goat at 11). For eternal merit, Viṣṇu specifies 'white-nosed rhinoceros' (vārdhrīṇasa) where Manu simply says 'rhinoceros.' Viṣṇu also lists specific foods to avoid at śrāddha (Vi_79.17–18): long pepper, mukundaka, eggplant, bottle-gourd, black gram, lentils, stale food, artificial salt, she-buffalo milk — prohibitions Manu does not enumerate.

**DAYS**

Manu (Mn_3.122, 3.276–3.281): Relatively general. The monthly śrāddha falls on the new moon (Piṇḍānvāhāryaka, Mn_3.122). Any day in the dark fortnight from the 10th onward (excluding the 14th) is praiseworthy (Mn_3.276). The 13th lunar day with honey is especially good (Mn_3.273). The śrāddha should be performed three times a year — in winter, summer, and the rainy season (Mn_3.281). The afternoon is preferred over the forenoon (Mn_3.278). Night, twilight, and sunrise are forbidden (Mn_3.280). No breakdown by day-of-week or nakṣatra, and no distinction between obligatory vs. optional occasions.

Viṣṇu (Vi_76–78): Highly specific. Chapter 76 lists OBLIGATORY (nitya) days: new moon, three aṣṭakā days (8th day of the dark fortnight in Mārgaśīrṣa, Pauṣa, and Māgha), three anvaṣṭakā days (the day after each aṣṭakā), the full moon of Māgha, the 13th day of the dark fortnight following the full moon of Prauṣṭhapada (Bhādrapada), and the ripening of rice and barley — failure to perform on these occasions incurs hell. Chapter 77 then lists OPTIONAL (kāmya) days: solar transit into a new sign, the two equinoxes, the two solstices, the Vyatīpāta conjunction, one's natal star, and a time of prosperity; an eclipse permits śrāddha even at twilight or night (Vi_77.8–9). Chapter 78 goes further still, mapping each day of the week (Sunday → health, Monday → good fortune, Tuesday → victory, Wednesday → all desires, Thursday → knowledge, Friday → wealth, Saturday → long life), each of the 27 nakṣatras to a specific boon, and each lunar tithi from 1 to 15 to a specific benefit; the 14th day is singled out for those killed in battle.

**WHERE VIṢṆU SPECIFIES WHAT MANU LEAVES GENERAL**

On foods: Manu groups all one-month plant foods into a single clause; Viṣṇu names five additional grain/pulse types (śyāmāka, priyaṅgu, wild rice, green gram, wheat), specifies which rhinoceros gives eternal merit, and provides a detailed list of prohibited foods. On days: Manu says only 'the dark fortnight from the 10th' and 'three times a year'; Viṣṇu creates a two-tier taxonomy (nitya vs. kāmya), names the specific aṣṭakā and anvaṣṭakā occasions by month, adds solar-astronomical events (solstices, equinoxes, transits, Vyatīpāta, eclipse), and enumerates specific fruits for every weekday, every nakṣatra, and every lunar tithi — none of which appear in Manu.

cases.prompt: Iqbal's 'Sair-e-Falak' speaker enters the heavens; the Wanderer's speaker contemplates a fallen earthly world. Pull what each speaker sees. Why does each see the cosmos this way — what about the speaker has determined the field of view?
cases.rationale: Cross-poem cosmic-view comparison forced to speaker-determines-view question.

sonnet.answer:
Sair-e-Falak (Bang-E-Dara Part 3, chapter 18) and The Wanderer (Old English Elegies, chapter 11) orient their speakers in opposite directions — one upward into the heavens, one earthward into ruin — and the difference is entirely a function of who each speaker is.

**What each speaker sees:**

The Sair-e-Falak speaker, companioned by Imagination, ascends through the 'cosmic blue' and breaks free from 'that old, weary system' of earthly cycles. He sees: stars gazing at his flight in wonder; Iram (the legendary garden-paradise); Tooba's bough with singing birds; unveiled Houris; the Saqian (celestial cupbearer) pouring drink; then, far from Paradise, a dark cold void — Hell — empty of Fire and Light, whose flames are 'borrowed' things. The angel Surosh reveals to him that souls bring their own embers from the lives they wrought.

The Wanderer's speaker is an exiled thane drifting over 'the rime-cold sea' and 'ice-bound waves.' He sees: frost and snow mingled with hail; sea-birds bathing; crumbling wine-halls; 'wind-blown walls' covered with frost; the ruins of civilization ('the old work of giants'); and the spectral absence of everything once present — 'Where has the horse gone? Where has the warrior gone? Where the treasure-giver?' His imagined clasping of his dead lord dissolves back into fallow waves and falling snow.

**Why each sees the cosmos this way:**

The Sair-e-Falak speaker looks upward because his constitutive faculty is Imagination — the poem literally names it as his guide. He is Iqbal's idealized spiritually free subject, unconstrained by the 'ring of dusk and dawn' (the mundane cycle of servitude and routine). His prophetic soul grants him vertical access: he can observe paradise and hell alike, and celestial revelation (Surosh) comes to him. The vision is expansive because the seer is internally elevated; his cosmos is luminous and revelatory because he is oriented by the active, ascending self.

The Wanderer's speaker looks downward because he is defined by loss and exile. His lord (the 'gold-friend') is dead, his hall is gone, his kinsmen are scattered — 'Wyrd is wholly steadfast' and has stripped him of every social anchor. This earthward condition is literal: he traverses icy seas and frozen plains, and when he tries to look inward (imagining his lord), he wakes to cold waves. What remains is only the evidence of decay. His wisdom — the 'ubi sunt' meditation on vanished horses and warriors — comes not from ascent but from accumulated suffering. He sees a world of transience and ruin because exile and bereavement have left him no other field of view; the crumbling 'enta geweorc' (the work of giants) mirrors his internal desolation.

In short: the Sair-e-Falak speaker's heavenward gaze is determined by spiritual freedom and prophetic imagination; the Wanderer's earthbound gaze is determined by social dispossession and the irreversibility of Wyrd. One is constituted by flight; the other by grief rooted to frozen ground.

cases.prompt: Compare Manu's discussion of stridhana (women's separate property) in chapter 9 with Yajnavalkya's treatment in chapter 2.
cases.rationale: Tests precise comparison of women's property rights across two foundational smritis.

sonnet.answer:
Both works address stridhana (a woman's separate property), but differ in scope, definition, and the protections afforded.

**Manu ch. 9 (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, chapter 9):**
Manu defines stridhana at Mn_9.194 as a 'sixfold' category: (1) what is given before the nuptial fire, (2) what is given at the bridal procession, (3) what is given as a token of affection, (4) what is received from her brother, (5) from her mother, and (6) from her father. Mn_9.195 adds that gifts given after marriage and by the husband out of affection belong to her offspring even if she predeceases her husband. Succession on a childless wife's death (Mn_9.196–197) depends on the form of marriage: in the Brahma, Daiva, Arsha, Gandharva, and Prajapatya forms it goes to the husband; in the Asura and other forms it goes to her mother and father. Mn_9.198 specifies that property given by a father to a daughter goes to her or her offspring. Crucially, Mn_9.199 restricts women's autonomy: 'Women should not make an expenditure from the family property which is common to many, nor even from their own wealth, without the permission of their husband.' Mn_9.200 further rules that ornaments worn during the husband's lifetime cannot be divided by heirs.

**Yajnavalkya ch. 2 (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6, chapter 2):**
Yajnavalkya defines stridhana at Yj_2.143 as: gifts from the father, mother, husband, or brother; what is received at the nuptial fire; and 'the gift on supersession (adhivédanika) and the like.' The definition is broader than Manu's because it explicitly includes the adhivédanika (the gift a husband gives a wife he is about to supersede with a second wife). Yj_2.145 addresses succession more granularly: in the four Brahma-type forms, a childless woman's stridhana goes to her husband; but if she has issue, it goes to her daughters — a daughter-protective rule absent from Manu. In the remaining (non-Brahma) forms it goes to her father. Yj_2.147 is notable for implicitly protecting women's ownership: a husband who has already taken stridhana need not return it only in four specific hardship cases (famine, religious duty, illness, imprisonment), implying that under ordinary circumstances he must return it. Yj_2.115 addresses the scenario where no stridhana was given at all: such wives should receive equal shares in any property partition. Yj_2.148 protects superseded wives specifically: they receive a gift of equal value to the missing stridhana, or half if some stridhana was already given.

**Key contrasts:**
1. *Scope of definition*: Manu lists six fixed sources; Yajnavalkya adds the gift on supersession and uses 'and the like,' suggesting an open-ended category.
2. *Daughters as heirs*: Yajnavalkya explicitly routes stridhana to daughters when there is issue from a Brahma-type marriage; Manu does not.
3. *Husband's access to stridhana*: Manu broadly subordinates the wife's spending to the husband's permission; Yajnavalkya frames the husband's use as temporary appropriation that must be returned except in hardship, implying stronger female ownership.
4. *Succession in lower-form marriages*: Manu sends it to 'mother and father'; Yajnavalkya says 'father' only.
5. *Superseded wife*: Only Yajnavalkya has an explicit protective rule (Yj_2.148) for wives displaced by a second marriage.

cases.prompt: Yajnavalkya gives a tighter, sutra-style code; Manu gives expansive verse. Pick the same procedural rule in both texts and lay them side by side.
cases.rationale: Tests rule-by-rule comparison demonstrating textual style.

sonnet.answer:
The shared procedural rule is the qualification and disqualification of witnesses in civil disputes (sākṣi-prakaraṇam / witness law).

**Yājñavalkya Smṛti, Chapter 2 (Vyavahārādhyāya), Yj_2.68–2.72** — tight, compressed sutra-style:

• Yj_2.68 (p-5c0bb3): Positive qualifications — ascetics, charitable men, men of good family, truth-tellers, those for whom dharma is paramount, upright men, those with sons, and those with wealth.
• Yj_2.69 (p-18320c): At least three witnesses, devoted to the rites of revelation and tradition; same caste and varṇa as the parties — or anyone may witness for anyone.
• Yj_2.70 (p-478abd): Disqualified — 'Women, children, the aged, gamblers, the intoxicated, the insane, those accused of a great sin, actors, heretics, forgers, and those with defective sense organs are not competent as witnesses.'
• Yj_2.71 (p-f5049f): Further disqualified — 'Outcastes, friends, those with a pecuniary interest, associates, enemies, thieves, perpetrators of violence, those convicted of a crime, and the banished.'
• Yj_2.72 (p-33a082): Exception — even one witness approved by both parties is acceptable; anyone can testify in cases of adultery, theft, assault, and violence.

Five verses. The rule is announced, affirmatively stated, negatively delimited, and closed with an exception — all with zero rhetorical padding.

**Manusmṛti, Chapter 8 (Chapter on Legal Procedure), Mn_8.61–8.72** — expansive verse form (all under corpus paragraph p-803bc3):

• Mn_8.61: Manu introduces the topic discursively — 'What kind of witnesses should be appointed by creditors in legal proceedings, such I will now declare, and how they should speak the truth.'
• Mn_8.62: 'Householders with sons, of established lineage, born of the Kshatriya, Vaishya, and Shudra varnas, when called by the plaintiff, are fit to give evidence, but not just anyone, except in a time of distress.'
• Mn_8.63: Trustworthy men from all varnas who know all the Dharmas and are not greedy.
• Mn_8.64: Exclusions — connected by wealth, close relatives, helpers, enemies, those with a proven fault, afflicted by disease, defamed.
• Mn_8.65: More exclusions — king, artisan, actor, śrotriya, religious-order wearer, one who has renounced.
• Mn_8.66: More exclusions — overly dependent, robber, those doing forbidden acts, old man, child, one alone, lowest caste, impaired senses.
• Mn_8.67: More exclusions — distressed, intoxicated, insane, hungry, thirsty, weary, lustful, angry, thief.
• Mn_8.68: Segregated procedure — women for women, twice-born for twice-born, Shudras for Shudras.
• Mn_8.69: Exception — anyone who witnessed something inside a house, in a forest, or in a matter of life and death may testify.
• Mn_8.70–8.72: Further nuances — emergency fallback witnesses, warning about children/sick, and the rule that in violence/theft/adultery cases witnesses should not be examined too strictly.

Twelve verses covering the same terrain as Yājñavalkya's five.

**Comparison**: Both texts share the identical legal logic — a positive definition of the ideal witness (householder, truthful, knowledgeable, not greedy), a tiered list of the disqualified (intoxicated, insane, interested party, enemy, actor, criminal, impaired), and an emergency exception for violent or secret crimes. Yājñavalkya's sutra-style compresses this into lean binary pairs: qualified vs. disqualified, general vs. exception. Manu re-expands each category into its own verse, adds procedural narrative ('I will now declare'), segregates by varna-of-the-parties (Mn_8.68), and multiplies sub-exceptions (Mn_8.70). The underlying rule is identical; the rhetorical shape is utterly different — Yājñavalkya gives a legislator's code, Manu gives a teacher's lecture.

cases.prompt: Iqbal mentions Hindi figures (Brahman, Ram, Hindostan, Hindu sages) in several poems. Where does he engage warmly and where polemically?
cases.rationale: Tests cross-volume tone tracking on a sensitive subject.

sonnet.answer:
Iqbal's engagement with Hindi figures in Bang-E-Dara divides clearly into warm and polemical registers:

**Warm engagement:**

1. **'Ram'** (Bang-E-Dara Part 3, ch. 17, slug: allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8): Iqbal writes with full-throated admiration. He calls Hindustan's philosophical tradition ('the Chalice of Hind with Wine of Truth') so profound that 'the West's philosophers are by its wisdom bound.' Ram is celebrated as 'Hind's Spiritual Guide,' and Hindustan can 'gaze with pride' on his existence. This is unambiguous praise.

2. **'Swami Ram Tirath'** (Bang-E-Dara Part 2, ch. 23, slug: allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c): A warm, admiring elegy for the Vedantic mystic Ram Tirath (1873–1906), who drowned himself in the Ganges as a form of spiritual self-annihilation. Iqbal maps Ram Tirath's act onto Sufi categories of fana (annihilation in God), calling him 'a pearl beyond price' and praising 'the negation of being' as a wonder. The tone is genuinely reverential — Iqbal admits he himself 'remains a prisoner' of the distinctions this mystic transcended.

3. **'Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet'** (Bang-E-Dara Part 1, ch. 26, slug: allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c): The 'National Song for Indian Children' warmly celebrates Hindustan as a shared homeland, mentioning Nanak's song of Oneness and the subcontinent's wisdom that 'left the wisest Greeks spellbound.' No polemic against Hindu traditions; it is pan-Indian and pluralist in spirit.

4. **'Himalaya'** (Bang-E-Dara Part 1, ch. 25, slug: allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c): Addresses the Himalaya as 'rampart of the realm of Hindustan' with warmth and wonder, celebrating the subcontinent's natural grandeur as a spiritual inheritance shared by all.

**Polemical/critical engagement:**

1. **'Sach keh doon ae Brahman!'** (Bang-E-Dara Part 1, ch. 36, slug: allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c): The very title ('Let me tell you the truth, O Brahmin, if you will not take offense') signals pointed critique. Iqbal charges that 'your ancient idols have lost reverence' and that the Brahmin learned to 'hold his kin in scorn' from them. He equally criticizes the Muslim Preacher ('the Preacher learned how war is born'). The critique is thus directed at institutionalized religion on both sides — the Brahmin's stone idols and the Preacher's dogmatism — but the Brahmin is specifically named and challenged. The poem ends with an invitation to common humanity rather than outright condemnation, so it is polemical-reconciliatory rather than purely adversarial.

2. **'Wataniyat'** (Bang-E-Dara Part 3, ch. 25, slug: allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8): While directed at nationalism as a concept rather than Hindu figures by name, this poem polemicizes against the 'idol of the Nation' as 'Faith's own final desecration.' Since territorial nationalism was in practice associated with a Hindu-majority conception of India, this poem is implicitly critical of that political framework, calling it a false god that 'lays waste the Prophet's house of faith.'

**Summary pattern:** Iqbal is consistently warm toward Hindu spiritual and philosophical figures (Ram as Hind's sage-guide, Swami Ram Tirath as a mystic who achieved fana), and toward Hindustan as a shared civilizational home (Himalaya, Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet). His polemic targets religious institutionalism (the Brahmin's stone idols) and, in his later phase, political Hinduism or territorial nationalism (Wataniyat). The split tracks the early-Iqbal pan-Indian nationalist versus later-Iqbal Islamic universalist trajectory within Bang-E-Dara's three parts.

cases.prompt: Comte Vol II opens with perfectionnement et dégradation; Vol I introduces a different framing. Pull both openings. Has the project's emphasis genuinely shifted, or is Vol II only sharpening what Vol I had already implied?
cases.rationale: Within-author project-evolution comparison.

sonnet.answer: Both volumes belong to Charles Comte's Traité de Législation. Vol I, chapter 1 (charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2, ch.1, p-6a39c9) opens with a methodological declaration: 'I wish simply to investigate the causes that make a people prosper or decline, or that render it stationary' — the entire chapter then defends the analytical/observational method against theological, philosophical, and jurisconsult rivals, leaving the meaning of 'prosperity' and 'decline' undefined. Vol II, chapter 1 (charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d, ch.1, p-d9a82b) opens by directly recalling that goal: 'The object of this work being to expound what are the principal causes of the prosperity and decadence of nations, it is suitable, before making this exposition, to determine the meaning one attaches to these words of decadence and prosperity.' It then systematically defines perfectionnement and dégradation at three levels — physical organs, intellectual faculties, and moral affections — before scaling up from individual to family, nation, and humanity. Vol II ch.1 also explicitly cites Vol I as the 'preceding book' in which man was already 'considered from three different points of view' (p-c5ad71), showing the continuity is deliberate. The emphasis has not genuinely shifted: Vol II is not a pivot but a completion of what Vol I's method demanded. Vol I announced the goal and established how to reason; Vol II does the definitional groundwork that the analytical method requires before causal claims can be made — you cannot observe causes of 'prosperity' without first fixing the meaning of the term. The perfectionnement/dégradation framing is the analytical method applied to anthropology, exactly as Vol I promised it would be applied to legislation. The arch is continuous; Vol II sharpens what Vol I had already implied.

cases.prompt: Juliana confined to a cell; the speaker of 'The Wife's Lament' confined under earth. Both are confinement scenes; the framings are very different. Pull the strongest line in each. Does either framing leave room for the other's mode of confinement?
cases.rationale: Cross-poem confinement-framing comparison.

sonnet.answer:
Both confinement scenes are in the corpus. Juliana's prison is in cynewulf-juliana-9a2157 (ch. 1, paragraph p-8666d0); the Wife's Lament earth-cave is in unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e (ch. 3, paragraph p-c1cc32).

**Strongest line — Juliana's cell (p-8666d0):**
"in that prison-house, the Savior of men, / shrouded in shadow. The Holy Spirit / was her constant companion."
The dungeon door is bolted shut by hammers, but the cell is immediately filled by the Holy Spirit. Physical confinement and spiritual habitation are simultaneous — the darkness of the prison becomes the condition of divine intimacy.

**Strongest line — Wife's Lament earth-cave (p-c1cc32):**
"under an oak tree in this earth-cave. / Ancient this earth-hall, I am filled with longing."
The space is called eorðscræf (earth-cave / earth-grave) — a natural hollow that doubles as a pre-burial space. She does not descend into heroism; she descends into the earth itself, alive, and waits.

**On whether either framing leaves room for the other's mode:**

Juliana's cell forecloses the Wife's mode almost entirely. The Holy Spirit as 'constant companion' leaves no room for the radical absence that defines the Wife's confinement — her earth-cave is structurally empty of comfort, divine presence, and response. The Wife's suffering is constituted by unanswered longing; Juliana's cell is constituted by an answered presence. A space filled with the Holy Spirit cannot hold the Wife's kind of despair.

The Wife's earth-cave framing leaves marginally more room. Both women are confined by external command — Juliana by a pagan judge, the Wife by her lord's bidding. But the Wife's mode has no spiritual adversary to wrestle (Juliana literally seizes the devil in her cell), no agonistic heroism, no upward trajectory. Juliana's mode — combat, testimony, triumph in extremis — has no surface to grip in an earth-cave defined by dim dales, briar-grown boroughs, and the endless sitting out of summer days alone. The Wife's framing can accommodate subjection to earthly authority, but not the interior liberation that makes Juliana's prison a sacred space.

cases.prompt: How does Iqbal's 'Tarana-e-Hindi' praise of Hindostan compare with the territorial-pride material in Cynewulf's Andreas (when Andrew speaks of his land)?
cases.rationale: Cross-tradition territorial-affection comparison.

sonnet.answer: The comparison cannot be substantiated from the corpus. Regarding Iqbal's 'Tarana-e-Hindi': the corpus does not contain a chapter explicitly titled 'Tarana-e-Hindi' in any Bang-e-Dara part. The closest match is 'Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet' (Bang-e-Dara Part 1, chapter 26, slug allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c), which does contain territorial-pride content praising Hindostan as a uniquely blessed land — invoking Chishti, Nanak, the Greeks, Persians, Tatars, and comparing India's peaks to Sinai, with the refrain 'This is my homeland, this land of mine.' Regarding Cynewulf's Andreas: the corpus version (cynewulf-andreas-07b573, chapter 1) does not contain any passage where Andrew speaks with territorial pride about his own land. The poem focuses on Andrew's Christian mission to Mermedonia. Achaia is mentioned only incidentally — once by God rebuking Andrew for his earlier reluctance ('as when in Achaia you made refusal'), and once at the end when Andrew prepares to return there. There is no speech in the corpus where Andrew celebrates or praises his homeland in a manner that could be compared to the territorial patriotism of the Tarana-e-Hindi / Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet. The premise of the question — that Andreas contains territorial-pride material 'when Andrew speaks of his land' — is not supported by the corpus.

Yajnavalkya chapter 3 is titled 'ON EXPIATION (prāyaścittādhyāya)' and begins with āśauca (ritual impurity), duties in distress, hermit duties, and an extended yoga/metaphysics section, but the chapter has a large internal gap: verse numbers jump from Yj_3.205 to Yj_3.327, omitting the range 206–326 where the specific offence-and-expiation rulings would be found. cases.prompt: 'Maa ka Khwām' and 'The Wife's Lament' are both women's intimate voices but differently constructed. Pull the most intimate line in each. Which voice is more constructed — that is, more visibly mediated by a male author imagining a female speaker?
cases.rationale: Cross-poem female-voice comparison forced to mediation question.

sonnet.answer:
Both poems are in the corpus.

'Maa ka Khwab' (Mother's Dream) is Bang-E-Dara Part 1, chapter 29 (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c). It is written by Allama Iqbal — a male poet who voices a mother. The most intimate line is: "My life! Where have you come, and left me all alone? Your absence leaves me in deep turmoil; I string each day a garland of my tears." (p-9c5ea5 / p-c4bed6)

'The Wife's Lament' is Old English Elegies, chapter 3 (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e). Its authorship is listed as Unknown. The most intimate line is: "we swore our vows, that naught but death alone could ever part us. Now all that is changed; it is now as if it never were, our love together." (p-66ceaf) — or alternatively: "there I can weep for my paths of exile, my many sorrows; for I can never find any rest from my heart's-care, nor from the longing that this life has brought me." (p-c1cc32)

Which voice is more visibly constructed / mediated by a male author imagining a female speaker? 'Maa ka Khwab' is. The reasons are structural, not merely biographical:

1. Attribution is certain: Iqbal is male. In 'The Wife's Lament' the author is anonymous.
2. In Iqbal's poem the mother's grief is allowed expression only to be corrected: the son turns away and tells her that her tears literally extinguish his lamp (spiritual progress). The female speaker's intimate sorrow is instrumentalized — it exists to deliver a didactic lesson about proper maternal detachment. The corrective child-voice is a male-authorial intrusion that redirects and disciplines the mother's lament.
3. The dream frame itself is a distancing device: the mother's emotion is filtered through a vision, then adjudicated by the son's speech.
4. In 'The Wife's Lament' no external voice intervenes to correct or moralize the speaker's grief. The woman's sorrow persists without being redirected toward a lesson. Even the poem's closing shift — imagining her beloved sitting under a stony slope — stays within the speaker's perspective rather than introducing a corrective authority.

Conclusion: 'Maa ka Khwab' (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c, ch. 29) is the more visibly constructed voice. The mother's intimacy is a set-up for a patriarchal-religious moral; the male author's presence is felt in the poem's architecture (grief → correction → silencing). 'The Wife's Lament' (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e, ch. 3), though likely composed in a monastery context, shows less visible mediation: the female speaker is granted uninterrupted interiority.

cases.prompt: Comte's Traité de la propriété Vol I and Viṣṇu Smṛti each treat ownership of land. Pull each one's foundational statement. What metaphysical assumption does each make about the relation of person to soil?
cases.rationale: Cross-tradition land-ownership metaphysics comparison.

sonnet.answer:
Both works are in the corpus. Here are their foundational statements on land ownership and the metaphysical assumptions each makes about the person-soil relationship.

**Comte, Traité de la propriété Vol I (charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99)**

Foundational statements (primarily Chapters 4, 5, and 9):
- Chapter 5 (p-e68210): 'The action of an organized being that unites to its own substance the things by means of which it grows, strengthens, and reproduces, is what we name appropriation.' Appropriation is a biological, natural act common to all living beings.
- Chapter 4 (p-7e2792): 'the appropriation, by nations, families, and individuals, of the various things on which human industry can be exercised, is a necessity of our nature' — property is not a legal convention but a law of nature.
- Chapter 9 (p-302405): 'In all positions, it is therefore by his labor that man appropriates the things he needs to exist.' And: 'the greatest part of the value that lands have among civilized nations is the result of the action that men have exercised upon them' — raw soil contributes only 1/1200th of the value cultivated land holds in France; the rest is the product of human industry.
- Chapter 9 (p-7b9327): 'the men who, first, put the land into cultivation... and who thus appropriated it to themselves, have snatched nothing from their fellow men.'

Metaphysical assumption: The person is an active, productive agent who creates virtually all the value in land through labor. The soil in its natural state has negligible worth; its value is almost entirely anthropogenic. The person-soil relationship is therefore **productive and transformative** — labor is what forges the moral bond between person and land. Ownership is grounded in this creative act, not in divine grant or political command. The soil is passive matter; the person activates it.

**Viṣṇu Smṛti (unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8)**

Foundational statements (primarily Chapters 1, 3, and 17):
- Chapter 1 (Vi_1.1–1.13): The earth (Vasudhā/Dhara) is a divine goddess, lifted from the cosmic ocean by Vishnu in Boar form and 'placed in a firm, fixed position' for the welfare of the worlds. The earth has subjectivity and asks Vishnu for support.
- Chapter 1 (Vi_1.47): Vishnu tells the Earth: 'Good people who delight in the practices of the varṇas and āśramas, devoted solely to the scriptures, They, O Dhara, will support you; the burden of you is placed upon them.' The earth is sustained by dharma-following humans — the relationship is cosmic duty, not productive transformation.
- Chapter 3 (Vi_3.22, 3.28): 'For tax, he shall take a sixth part of the grain annually from his subjects' and 'the king receives a sixth part of the good and evil deeds of his subjects.' The king's entitlement to land's produce is tied to his cosmic role as protector — his governance IS the mechanism by which the earth's bounty flows.
- Chapter 3 (Vi_3.81–3.83): 'he should grant land to Brāhmaṇas' with documented titles; 'he shall not confiscate land granted by another.' Private land-holding is mediated by royal sanction; the king is the source of legitimate land tenure.
- Chapter 17 (Vi_17.13): 'In his absence [no heirs], it goes to the king, except for the property of a Brāhmaṇa.' The king is the residual owner of all non-Brahminical property.

Metaphysical assumption: The earth is a sacred divine entity under cosmic governance, not passive matter awaiting transformation. The person-soil relationship is **custodial and hierarchically ordered** — humans hold land not because they created value in it but because they occupy a position in the dharmic hierarchy (varna, āśrama) ratified by the king's protection. The king takes a sixth part because his protection is the cosmic condition under which the earth's sustenance flows to anyone. Land grants flow downward from Vishnu → dharmic order → king → subjects. The assumption is that **cosmic order (dharma), not labor**, constitutes the person's claim to soil.

**Contrast**: Comte assumes the person and soil are linked by productive transformation — labor creates value and therefore ownership. The Viṣṇu Smṛti assumes the person and soil are linked by cosmic hierarchy — dharmic duty and royal mediation create the condition under which soil may be held. For Comte, the soil is ontologically inert until the person acts on it; for the Viṣṇu Smṛti, the soil is ontologically divine and the person receives access to it by occupying their proper place in a divinely-ordered social cosmos.

cases.prompt: Comte argues ancient slavery distorts later jurisprudence. The smriti tradition has elaborate provisions for dāsa and dāsī. Pull each side. Are Comte and the smritis describing the same phenomenon at different distances, or different phenomena under one English word?
cases.rationale: Cross-tradition slavery comparison forced to identity-of-phenomenon test.

sonnet.answer:
Comte's argument (Traité de la propriété Vol. I, ch. 1) is specifically about how Roman/Greek jurisprudence was distorted by the institution of chattel slavery: jurists like Papinian, Paul, and Ulpian were slave-owners who could not ground rights and duties in human nature without contradicting their practice. They therefore had to build legal foundations on principles they could not demonstrate, leaving jurisprudence permanently compromised. Comte frames this as a philosophical-theoretical problem — a contradiction between professed natural liberty (jus naturale) and the practice of treating persons as property — that contaminates all subsequent legal thinking that inherits Roman doctrine.

The smriti tradition, by contrast, provides an elaborate internal jurisprudence of dāsa rather than a critique of it. The Nāradasmṛti (ch. 1, Nar. 5.02–5.42) identifies fifteen categories of dāsa, differentiated by mode of entry: born in the house, purchased, received as gift, inherited, maintained during a famine, pledged by master, freed from great debt, captured in battle, won in a wager, self-submitted, apostate from asceticism, time-limited, slave for food, enslaved for a female slave, and self-sold. Crucially, each category has distinct exit conditions: hereditary slaves (first four) are released only by master's grace or by saving the master's life; debt-slaves are released on repayment; battle-captives on providing a substitute; and Nar. 5.36 explicitly declares that those 'abducted by thieves and sold, and those enslaved by force — they must be freed by the king; slavery is not intended for them.' The Manusmṛti (ch. 8, Mn. 8.413–8.415) lists seven categories and adds a structurally distinct claim: the Shudra is a natural servant whose servitude is innate and cannot be removed even by manumission.

Are they describing the same phenomenon at different distances, or different phenomena under one English word?

The answer is primarily: different phenomena, with a zone of overlap. Several diagnostics:

1. Forced enslavement: Nar. 5.36's declaration that forced enslavement is illegitimate and must be undone by the king is irreconcilable with Roman law's acceptance of war captives as slaves — one of the central sources Comte identifies. The most morally contested category in Rome is actively delegitimized in the Nāradasmṛti.

2. Caste-as-servitude: Manu's claim that a Shudra cannot be freed from servitude because it is 'innate' (Mn. 8.414) describes something Roman law has no counterpart for — a cosmic-order-based permanent condition that precedes and survives any contractual or conquest-based legal status. This is not slavery in Comte's sense (legally acquired property in a person) but a dharmic category that English translates as servitude for lack of a better word.

3. Temporal and conditional forms: The smriti system has many time-limited and condition-based forms (debt-slave released on payment, famine-slave released by two oxen, food-slave released by master's failure to provide food). These are closer to indentured servitude or debt peonage than to the Roman chattel model Comte analyzes.

4. The distortion argument: Comte's critique targets the specific contradiction in Roman jurisprudence between professing natural liberty as the foundation of rights while denying it to slaves. The smriti tradition does not have this contradiction — it does not claim natural liberty as a universal baseline. It builds a hierarchical cosmic order in which different beings have different prescribed roles. There is therefore no distortion of the kind Comte identifies: the smriti is internally consistent in its own terms, even if its terms are inegalitarian.

Zone of genuine overlap: The four permanent hereditary categories in Nārada (born in house, purchased, received as gift, inherited — Nar. 5.24, 5.27) are structurally close to Roman chattel slavery. For this subset, Comte's analysis does apply: a jurisprudence built around persons-as-inheritable-property faces the same foundational contradiction. This is the narrow sense in which they describe the same phenomenon.

Conclusion: The smriti dāsa system and Comte's ancient slavery are not the same phenomenon at different distances. The smriti term dāsa covers a spectrum from hereditary chattel (overlapping with Comte's target) to debt peonage to cosmic-order-based Shudra service — categories English collapses under 'slave' or 'servant' but which have distinct legal logics. Comte's critique of jurisprudential distortion applies most directly to the hereditary/purchased subset within the smriti scheme, but is structurally deflected by the smriti's dharmic framing, which avoids the natural-liberty contradiction by never claiming universal natural liberty in the first place.

cases.prompt: Manu uses 12 chapters; Yājñavalkya uses three structural divisions (ācāra, vyavahāra, prāyaścitta). Pull each architecture and ask what the simplification gains in clarity and what it costs in cosmological framing.
cases.rationale: Cross-text architectural comparison forced to gain-vs-cost call.

sonnet.answer:
Both texts are in the corpus: Manusmṛti (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, 12 chapters) and Yājñavalkya Smṛti (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6, 3 chapters).

**Manu's 12-chapter architecture:**
- Ch. 1: Full cosmogony — creation of the universe from the Self-existent One (primordial darkness → golden egg → elements → cosmic time: yugas, manvantaras, Brahma's day/night cycle → varnas emanating from Brahma's body-parts) + table of contents for the entire work
- Ch. 2: Sources of dharma + brahmacharya (student life)
- Chs. 3–5: Householder duties (marriage, livelihood, purification, food, women)
- Ch. 6: Forest hermit / renunciant
- Ch. 7: Kingship — king constituted from particles of Indra, Yama, Agni, Varuṇa, etc. (Mn 7.4–7.7), making royal authority a cosmic function
- Ch. 8: Civil legal procedure
- Ch. 9: Women, inheritance, criminal law
- Ch. 10: Mixed castes, duties in distress
- Ch. 11: Prāyaścitta (penances)
- Ch. 12: Karma theory, the three guṇas (sattva/rajas/tamas), transmigration, ātmajñāna as the highest good, final return to the Self — closing cosmological/soteriological frame

The 12-chapter scheme follows the biographical arc of the āśramas (student → householder → hermit → renunciant) embedded within a cosmic frame, bookended by cosmogony (ch. 1) and soteriology (ch. 12).

**Yājñavalkya's three-division architecture:**
- Ch. 1 (ācāra, 'Chapter on Conduct'): Opens with a hermeneutical list of 14 sources of dharma (Purāṇas, Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, Dharmaśāstra, and the six Vedic aṅgas — Yj 1.3–1.5), then proceeds through student life, householder duties, and renunciation. No cosmogony.
- Ch. 2 (vyavahāra, 'Chapter on Legal Procedure'): Opens immediately with the king appointing learned Brāhmaṇas to adjudicate disputes (Yj 2.1–2.3). Covers court composition, lawsuit procedure, debt, property, inheritance, witnesses.
- Ch. 3 (prāyaścitta, 'Chapter on Expiation'): Begins with ritual impurity (death, birth) → penances for specific sins → karma, guṇas, transmigration → detailed anatomy of the body (bones, veins, organs: Yj 3.75–3.104) → Self-knowledge/yoga (Yj 3.141–3.204) → the two paths (devayana/pitṛyana, Yj 3.193–3.196) → liberation. The text closes with sages praising Yājñavalkya.

**What the simplification gains in clarity:**
1. Functional tripartition: The three heads — what one must do (ācāra), how disputes are resolved (vyavahāra), how violations are remedied (prāyaścitta) — are logically distinct domains of dharmic life. A practitioner or commentator can navigate directly to the relevant section without wading through the biographical structure.
2. Autonomous legal domain: Yājñavalkya elevates civil/legal procedure to a full independent division. Manu distributes legal material across chs. 7 (kingship), 8 (civil law), and 9 (women/criminal/family) — three chapters whose overlap makes synthesis difficult. The unity of Yājñavalkya's vyavahāra enabled the Mitākṣarā (Vijñāneśvara's commentary) to become the dominant legal authority for most of India.
3. Eliminates life-stage sprawl: Collapsing Manu's chs. 2–6 into a single ācāra division strips the biographical-sequential logic (the āśrama arc) and yields a more topic-focused, referee-friendly reference.

**What it costs in cosmological framing:**
1. No opening cosmogony: Manu ch. 1 derives the binding force of dharma from cosmic creation itself — varnas emerge from Brahma's mouth, arms, thighs, and feet (Mn 1.31); the norms are not merely traditional but ontologically grounded. Yājñavalkya replaces this with a hermeneutical foundation (14 sources), shifting from ontological to epistemological justification. Dharma is authoritative because authoritative texts say so, not because it flows from creation.
2. Cosmic time lost: Manu's account of the four yugas (Mn 1.81–1.86) explicitly ties dharmic prescription to cosmic era — tapas is paramount in the Kṛta age, knowledge in the Tretā, sacrifice in the Dvāpara, giving in the Kali age. This explains why norms differ across time. Yājñavalkya drops this frame, presenting dharma as essentially ahistorical and non-cyclical.
3. King demythologized: Manu's king is literally assembled from divine particles (Mn 7.4–7.7) — he is Agni, Yama, Indra, Varuṇa in human form. This cosmological grounding makes royal authority sacred. Yājñavalkya opens vyavahāra with the king as an institutional judge who must control anger and greed (Yj 2.1) — an administrator, not a god. Legal authority is rationalized, not mythologized.
4. Soteriology deferred, not framing: Manu brackets the entire normative corpus between cosmogony (ch. 1) and ātmajñāna-as-highest-good (ch. 12), so every rule of conduct sits explicitly within a cosmic and soteriological purpose. Yājñavalkya's karma/guṇas/yoga/liberation section (Yj 3.135–3.204) is present but is a terminal sequence within prāyaścitta, not a bookending frame. The cosmos becomes a conclusion rather than a container — visible at the end but absent as architecture.

In sum: Yājñavalkya's tripartition is a juristic rationalization. It achieves clarity by separating conduct, adjudication, and remediation as distinct functional domains, but it sacrifices the mythic-cosmological scaffolding that gives Manu its sense of dharma as embedded in the very fabric of cosmic creation and cosmic time.

cases.prompt: Comte's chapters arguing against religion as the foundation of legislation (Vol I ch. 8) and against legislation grounded in scripture (ch. 9) form a cluster. What's the cumulative claim?
cases.rationale: Within-volume cluster reading.

sonnet.answer:
The cumulative claim across chapters 8 and 9 of Charles Comte's Traité de Législation Vol I (charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2) is that no a priori or transcendent principle — whether religious/scriptural authority or a duty-based moral axiom — can validly ground legislation. Each chapter attacks a different species of non-empirical foundation:

Chapter 8 ('Du système qui fait d'une religion positive le fondement exclusif de la morale et des lois') argues that grounding legislation exclusively in a positive religion's precepts is harmful on multiple counts: (1) it immunizes laws from empirical critique, (2) it freezes societies at the moment of the religion's formulation, perpetuating whatever errors it contained, (3) it hands unchecked power to a clerical class with self-interested motives, (4) and — crucially — no religion itself actually prescribes such a system; it is a political construction by priests and rulers, not a divine requirement.

Chapter 9 ('De la doctrine qui fonde la morale et la législation sur le principe de l'utilité') extends the critique to Bentham's utilitarian method. Comte accepts the principle of utility as correct, but argues that Bentham's methodological error was framing it as a duty ('public happiness *ought to be* the object of the legislator'). Starting from an 'ought' rather than an observed fact invites the same problem as scripture: when challenged — 'why ought?' — one cannot demonstrate the duty without either circular reasoning or ultimately appealing to a transcendent source. Comte makes this explicit: if the interlocutor won't accept the utility principle as self-evident, 'where then to find the reason for the duty, if one does not wish to resort to the book of Mohammed?'

Taken together, the two chapters make a single cumulative claim: legislation cannot be founded on any principle that precedes and immunizes itself from empirical observation — whether that principle is divine revelation, clerical dogma, or an axiomatic moral duty. Both theocratic and duty-based-utilitarian approaches share the fatal flaw of requiring prior acceptance of a premise that cannot itself be demonstrated from facts. The only legitimate foundation for legislation is the purely positive, analytical method: observing human nature and the consequences of laws and institutions, expounding facts and their causal connections, and letting rules emerge from that evidence rather than being deduced from normative axioms.

cases.prompt: Iqbal's 'Wataniyat' rejects territorial nationalism; Comte argues territorial nation-building can produce moral progress. Pull each operative claim. Where is the disagreement substantive, and where is it just a difference of vocabulary?
cases.rationale: Cross-author territorial-politics comparison forced to substantive-vs-vocabulary call.

sonnet.answer:
Both texts are in the corpus. Iqbal's 'Wataniyat' is Bang-E-Dara Part 3, chapter 25; the relevant Comte works are Charles Comte's Traité de la propriété Vol I (chapters 7–8) and Traité de Législation Vols II–III.

**Iqbal's operative claims ('Wataniyat', allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8, ch. 25):**
1. Territorial nationalism is the 'greatest idol' of the modern age — it 'wears Faith's own final desecration as its robe.'
2. It lays waste to the Prophet's house of faith and Islamic unity.
3. The state defines the nation as land; the Prophet defines homeland as divine command (umma, not territory): 'In the discourse of the state, the Nation is but land; in the Prophet's teaching, Homeland is by God's command.'
4. Territorial nationalism sows inter-communal rivalry, drives conquest, makes politics flee from truth, and tears apart 'God's own creation.'
5. It shears the roots of Islamic brotherhood — Muslims should be a transnational community, not bounded by soil.

**Charles Comte's operative claims (charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99, chs. 7–8; charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d and vol-iii):**
1. Each nation has a natural territorial property — the highest and most encompassing form of property (ch. 7, p-2cfb18).
2. Natural limits (mountains, seas, rivers) determine the proper territorial boundaries of peoples; nations organized within these natural limits prosper, while artificial territorial arrangements produce strife (ch. 8, p-55b726).
3. As a nation develops intellectually and materially, its territorial property becomes more incontestable: 'the more a nation develops... the more the territory it occupies becomes for it an incontestable and uncontested property' (ch. 7, p-0a6e26).
4. Local territorial circumstances (soil, climate, configuration) shape the physical, intellectual, and moral development of peoples — natural territorial organization is the precondition for civilizational progress (Traité de Législation Vol III, ch. 42).
5. Artificial territorial divisions imposed by dynastic ambition are contrary to nature and retard moral and civilizational progress; the solution is not to abandon territorial identity but to align political units with natural geographical limits.

**Where the disagreement is substantive:**
1. *The proper unit of moral community.* For Iqbal, the correct locus of collective identity is the transnational umma (faith-community defined by divine command); territorial location is irrelevant — 'To be confined to one place is calamity.' For Comte, the natural and morally productive unit is precisely the territorially bounded people. This is a genuine, deep disagreement: one makes territorial identity the foundation of social progress; the other treats it as an idol that corrupts genuine moral solidarity.
2. *The moral valence of territorial attachment.* Iqbal treats wataniyat as a form of idolatry that destroys Islamic brotherhood and drives conquest. Comte treats territorial property as a natural right, and respect for it as the basis of inter-national justice. They contradict each other directly on whether territorial nationalism is morally corrupting or morally foundational.
3. *The direction of moral progress.* Iqbal implies that moral progress for Muslims means transcending territorial particularity toward a universal Islamic community. Comte argues moral progress follows the consolidation and naturalization of territorial organization — progress moves toward better-bounded, more stable territorial nations, not beyond them.

**Where it is merely vocabulary:**
1. *'Natural.'* Both appeal to what is 'natural' — Iqbal to the divine/natural order of the Islamic umma, Comte to physical geography. The shared term 'natural' masks a real disagreement about what nature or divine command requires. This is not a genuine convergence.
2. *Criticism of artificial imposition.* Iqbal criticizes territorial nationalism as an alien European import ('this new age's philosophy'). Comte criticizes dynastic territorial divisions as contrary to the nature of things. Both reject artificial arrangements — but Iqbal's deeper objection is to any territorial basis for collective identity, not just artificial territorial divisions. Comte would accept that bad territorial boundaries should be reformed into good natural ones; Iqbal would reject the premise that territory should define collective identity at all. The shared anti-artificial vocabulary disguises this difference.
3. *'Progress.'* Both believe in civilizational improvement. But the shared word conceals incompatible content: for Comte, progress means better territorial organization; for Iqbal, it means return to universal Islamic brotherhood that transcends territory.

**Summary:** The disagreement is primarily substantive. The core dispute is whether moral solidarity properly inheres in a territorially bounded natural community (Comte) or in a transnational faith community that cannot be reduced to land (Iqbal). Both use the language of 'nature,' 'progress,' and 'homeland,' but these shared terms do not create genuine overlap — they mask the deeper incompatibility. The only area of superficial convergence is a shared rejection of 'artificial' territorial arrangements, but this convergence dissolves because they disagree about what a non-artificial arrangement would look like.

cases.prompt: 'The Ruin' contemplates a fallen Roman city without naming it. Iqbal's 'Saqaliya' names Sicily. Find the most charged line in each. What does naming or refusing-to-name commit each speaker to?
cases.rationale: Cross-tradition grief-of-empire comparison forced to a naming question.

sonnet.answer:
Both poems are in the corpus.

**The Ruin** appears as chapter 16 of 'Old English Elegies' (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e). The most charged line is: "until mighty Wyrd overturned it all" (paragraph p-ba51da). This is the poem's climactic agent-assignment — the only force named in the entire poem is Wyrd (fate), not any conqueror, emperor, or city. The fallen place has no name; even its destroyers are unnamed. The speaker stands outside history as a pure witness.

**Saqaliya** appears as chapter 21 of 'Bang-E-Dara Part 2' (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c). The most charged line is the opening couplet: "Weep now, unburden your heart, O eye that rains down tears of blood, / For there, in sight, lies the grave of Hejazi civilization." (paragraph p-08760e). By the time Iqbal reaches the explicit apostrophe "Ah, O Sicily! You are the honor of the sea" (p-379aeb), Sicily is already loaded: it has been named as the burial ground of the Hejaz — of Islam's originary civilization.

**What naming/refusing-to-name commits each speaker to:**

The Old English poet's refusal to name commits the speaker to a position of aesthetic detachment and universal impermanence. The ruined city is described only as 'the work of giants' — alien, pre-Christian, inscrutable. Wyrd, not any identifiable enemy or people, is the agent of destruction. This anonymity forecloses political mourning: the grief is elegiac and formal, not partisan. The speaker does not own this history or claim inheritance from it. The refusal to name turns the poem into a meditation on mutability itself — any city, every city.

Iqbal's naming commits him to political inheritance and obligation. Naming Sicily inserts it into a chain of Islamic losses — Baghdad (mourned by Hafez of Shiraz), Jahanabad (mourned by Dagh), Granada (mourned by Ibn Badrun), now Sicily (mourned by Iqbal). Each mourner is also named. To name the place is to claim it, to indict the present, and to mobilize grief: "I shall carry you as a gift to Hindustan; I weep here myself, to make others weep there" (p-665fb2). The naming creates an inheritance that makes mourning into a political act — grief as propaganda, grief as continuity. The anonymous Old English speaker can only witness; Iqbal's named Sicily demands a response.

cases.prompt: Take the closing chapter of a Comte volume — say the final chapter of Traité de Législation Vol II — and place it beside the closing chapter of a Dunoyer volume. Whose ending is more programmatic?
cases.rationale: Closing-chapter comparison across the two French authors. Tests last-chapter retrieval without committing to a specific (and possibly mis-numbered) chapter index.

sonnet.answer:
Dunoyer's ending is decisively more programmatic. Comparing the two:

**Comte, Traité de Législation Vol II, Chapter 27** (work_slug: charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d) is a purely descriptive ethnographic chapter surveying the social organization and moral conditions of African peoples (Congo, western coast between the tropics vs. the Cape). Its final paragraphs draw comparative observations — that fertile land produces greater subjugation, that agricultural peoples face slavery rather than extermination, that harsh subordination is a lesser evil than constant savage warfare — but offer no prescriptive agenda, no reform call, no forward-looking program. It ends as it begins: as careful cross-cultural analysis with no directive content.

**Dunoyer, Nouveau traité d'économie Vol I, Chapter 12** (work_slug: charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce) ends with a formal 'Conclusion:' section laying out four explicit propositions about social states: (1) perfect equality is impossible; (2) the current coexistence of growing opulence and growing misery is real; (3) a state where misery is contained and well-being spreads is 'realizable, but not yet realized: it is the state toward which we must tend'; (4) a state without any miserable people is distant but not contradictory with nature. This is a manifesto structure — a graded program pointing society toward a specific realizable goal.

**Dunoyer, Nouveau traité d'économie Vol II, Chapter 19** (work_slug: charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03) is even more expansive in its prescriptive content: it calls for the wholesale reform of intellectual education (abandon classical languages, orient schools toward the arts men actually practice), demands the abolition of government monopolies over printing and teaching, and closes with quantitative evidence of intellectual progress as an argument for removing all remaining obstacles. The final sentence — 'there will be no progress that does not serve; a people will not have perfected a single one of the means of labor in its application to the culture of intelligences without one seeing this culture act with more power' — is a forward-looking principle implying an ongoing agenda of liberalization.

In short: Comte's chapter 27 closes as ethnographic inventory; both Dunoyer closing chapters close as reform programs. Dunoyer Vol I is the more formally programmatic (explicit four-point conclusion with a prescribed direction); Dunoyer Vol II is the more substantively programmatic (a comprehensive agenda for intellectual liberty and educational reform). Either way, Dunoyer is far more programmatic than Comte.

cases.prompt: Manu and Yājñavalkya both treat vācika sins — sins of speech. Pull each text's rules. What gets named as a sin of speech in one text but not the other, and what does that asymmetry tell you about the social anxieties of each text?
cases.rationale: Cross-text speech-sin comparison forced to social-anxiety reading.

sonnet.answer:
Both Manu and Yājñavalkya enumerate the same canonical four vācika (verbal) sins in their karma-cosmology sections. Manu (Mn_12.6, unknown-manusmrti-347b76 ch. 12) lists: harshness, falsehood, slander of all kinds, and irrelevant talk. Yājñavalkya (Yj_3.135, unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6 ch. 3) lists: untruth, slander, harsh speech, and idle talk — effectively the same four items in different order.

The asymmetry emerges when each text's fuller treatment is examined:

IN MANU BUT NOT YĀJÑAVALKYA: Manu's penance chapter (Mn_11.55, ch. 12 file) elevates three specific verbal acts to the most severe tier — equal in gravity to brahmin-slaying: (1) false boasting (claiming ritual/Vedic credentials one does not possess), (2) slander that reaches the king (politically escalated defamation), and (3) false accusation against a guru. A second tier (Mn_11.56, [p-77c6f2]) names reviling the Veda and giving false testimony as verbal sins equivalent to drinking liquor. None of these appear in Yājñavalkya's penance chapter (ch. 3) as named verbal sins with specific penalties.

IN YĀJÑAVALKYA BUT NOT MANU: Yājñavalkya's conduct chapter (Yj_1.33, ch. 1, [p-6a6d01]) names obscene speech as a prohibited verbal act for the student, alongside slander. This category does not appear in Manu's formal verbal sin lists.

What the asymmetry tells us about social anxieties:

Manu's unique verbal sins are all hierarchically and politically charged. False boasting protects the exclusivity of Brahmanical status — in a world where ritual credentials conferred enormous social capital, fraudulent speech claims threatened the entire varna order. Slander that reaches the king protects royal authority as a second pillar of the social structure; Manu is deeply invested in the brahmin-king dyad. False accusation of a guru protects the guru-disciple relationship as the transmission mechanism of Vedic knowledge. Reviling the Veda and false testimony extend this anxiety: the Veda is cosmic truth, testimony is social truth, and both must be inviolate. Manu's anxiety is about speech as a social weapon that can erode hierarchy, corrupt power, and destabilize the ritual order from which Brahmanical authority flows.

Yājñavalkya's unique addition (obscene speech) reflects a different register of anxiety: the ritual-spiritual purity of the student's inner world. The brahmacharī must avoid words that arouse desire because sexual impurity is ritual impurity. This fits Yājñavalkya's overall orientation as a more yogically-inclined text — Yājñavalkya is repeatedly styled 'the lord of yogis' and chapter 3 culminates in liberation philosophy, not social-legal taxonomy. The anxiety here is not about speech destabilizing the social order but about speech contaminating the self that seeks liberation.

cases.prompt: Iqbal's 'Larkiyan Parh Rahi Hain Angrezi' (girls reading English, Bang-e-Dara Part 3 ch. 27) is a critical poem. Compare its tone with Iqbal's 'Bachay ki Dua' on early education.
cases.rationale: Tests within-author pedagogy contrast.

sonnet.answer:
Both poems are found in the corpus. 'Larkiyan Parh Rahi Hain Angrezi' is Bang-e-Dara Part 3, ch. 27 (slug: allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8); 'Bachay Ki Dua' is Bang-e-Dara Part 1, ch. 16 (slug: allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c).

Tonal comparison:

'Larkiyan Parh Rahi Hain Angrezi' (Part 3, ch. 27) is a short, epigrammatic satirical poem (classified under 'Zarifana' — the humorous/satirical section in Iqbal's own scheme). Its tone is sardonic and ironic. The opening couplet — 'The girls are learning English now / The nation has found the path to salvation' — is deliberately sarcastic: equating English-language education with national salvation is precisely what Iqbal is mocking. The poem continues: 'The Western style is held in view / Eastern customs are deemed a sin.' The critique is not anti-education but anti-mimicry — the uncritical adoption of colonial culture at the cost of Eastern identity. The poem closes with suspended, foreboding irony: 'What scene will this drama show? / The gaze awaits the curtain to rise.' The outcome is withheld, but the tone signals dread rather than hope. The poem is pointed, politically charged, and biting.

'Bachay Ki Dua' (Part 1, ch. 16) is a devotional prayer in a child's voice and could not be more different in register. The tone is tender, luminous, and aspirational. The child prays: 'O God, may my life be like a candle's flare' and 'love the candle of knowledge, and draw ever nigher' — education here is figured as a moth drawn to the light of knowledge, a spiritual and self-elevating act. The poem is oriented toward service: illuminate the world, adorn the homeland (like a flower adorns a garden), champion the poor and weak. It closes in confident supplication, with no antagonism to any cultural identity.

The key tonal contrast: 'Bachay Ki Dua' treats learning as inner illumination and national-ethical vocation — warm, private, hopeful. 'Larkiyan Parh Rahi Hain Angrezi' treats a specific form of education (English schooling for girls as colonial assimilation) as cultural capitulation — public, ironic, apprehensive. In the first poem, the candle of knowledge is something to love; in the second, Western-style schooling is a drama whose curtain is feared. The divergence also reflects the two parts of Bang-e-Dara: Part 1 is more didactic and devotional; Part 3 is more politically satirical and socially critical. Both poems engage education, but from opposite rhetorical positions — one the intimate aspiration of a child, the other the cutting gaze of a cultural critic.

cases.prompt: 'The Wanderer' and 'The Ruin' both stand among the wreckage of older orders. Iqbal in 'Saqaliya' does the same for Muslim Sicily. Pick the speaker whose ruin is most thoroughly past — and say how you can tell.
cases.rationale: Cross-tradition ruin-contemplation comparison forced to a temporal-distance ranking.

sonnet.answer:
The speaker of 'The Ruin' (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e, ch.16) stands before the most thoroughly past ruin. Here is how you can tell:

1. No living connection to what fell. The Ruin's speaker is a nameless beholder with zero personal or genealogical stake in the destroyed city. The builders are 'in the ground's hard grip, till a hundred generations of people have passed' — the poem explicitly sets the fall in the deep-remote past. The speaker can only observe, never grieve as an heir.

2. Purely archaeological mode. Every sentence is third-person past-tense historical description: 'Bright were the city-halls... death took all the sword-brave men.' There is no first-person 'I', no dreamed-of reunion, no ongoing quest for a replacement patron — just a witness cataloguing ruins.

3. The poem enacts its own subject: the manuscript is physically decayed, leaving multiple [...] lacunae mid-sentence. Even the text is a ruin.

By contrast, The Wanderer (ch.11) has a speaker still alive in his exile, still dreaming of clasping his dead lord's knees, still searching the sea for 'a giver of treasure' — the loss is recent and its effects actively ongoing. His ruin is not yet past; it is the condition of his present.

And in Saqaliya (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c, ch.21), Iqbal explicitly identifies himself as an heir: 'To sorrow-fated Iqbal, your mourning was bequeathed' and 'I am but the dust of that caravan for which you were the destination.' He plans to 'carry you as a gift to Hindustan' — the ruin of Muslim Sicily is alive in his consciousness and actively being transmitted. This is the opposite of a thoroughly past ruin.

So: The Ruin's ruin is most thoroughly past — marked by anonymous spectatorship, a hundred-generation temporal gulf, the absence of any surviving heir, and a poem that is itself fragmentary.
