cases.prompt: Andreas describes God's intervention; Manu's book 1 describes Brahmā's act of creation. Pull each foundational moment. Both are theological openings, but the theologies differ — what does each text need its god to be capable of?
cases.rationale: Cross-tradition theological-opening comparison forced to god-capability question.

sonnet.answer:
**Andreas (cynewulf-andreas-07b573, chapter 1):** The foundational moment of God's intervention comes in two moves. First, Matthew cries out from his Mermedonian prison and God answers directly: 'Hold fast, my Matthew! Do not lose heart. I shall not leave you lost in this torment, nor forsake My own. I shall free you from bondage... I will send Andrew to save you from sorrow' (p-bdbda7). Second, God commands Andrew on the shoreline and then disguises himself as a ship captain — 'They were the Lord Himself, the Almighty King, with his angels two, disguised in the likeness of lonely shipmen' (p-24dbdc) — ferrying Andrew toward Mermedonia while concealing his identity.

**Manusmrti, Book 1 (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, chapter 1):** The foundational moment is the cosmogony narrated in verses Mn_1.5–1.26. The universe begins in formless darkness: 'This universe existed as darkness, unperceived, without distinctive marks, unknowable, as if wholly asleep on all sides' (p-61df11). The Self-existent One (Svayambhū) then manifests himself, creates the waters through contemplation, and casts his seed into them (p-54fad8). The seed becomes a golden egg from which Brahmā is born (p-153021). He then splits the egg to form heaven and earth (p-cd6713, p-3bd729), generates mind, ego, and the five sense elements from himself (p-f689c4), assigns names and actions to all beings (p-3a6c83), and separates Dharma from non-Dharma (p-84ef7f).

**What each text needs its god to be capable of:**

Andreas needs a God of *personal intervention and historical presence*. He must be able to hear individual prayer and respond in real time, issue commands to specific followers, disguise himself in human form, and orchestrate rescue across geography and time. His power is relational and narrative — it operates through conversation, disguise, and commission. The poem's theology demands a God who is *in* history, managing it from within, responding to the faithful rather than simply setting the cosmos running.

Manusmrti needs a God of *ontological self-sufficiency and cosmogonic generativity*. Brahmā is not summoned by prayer; he self-originates from within formless matter, creates through contemplation (not command to another), and generates the very categories of existence — space, time, dharma, the Vedas, species names and distinct duties. His power is structural: he produces the framework within which all subsequent existence is intelligible. The text demands a God who is complete unto himself, who creates not just beings but the distinctions and norms by which beings are organized.

In short: Andreas needs a god who responds; Manusmrti needs a god who generates. One theology is built on rescue within a fallen situation; the other is built on the self-producing ground of all situation.

cases.prompt: Manu sees Brahmins as primary recipients of dakṣiṇā; Comte sees property as deriving from labour. Pull each foundational claim. Where do these two traditions clash on what makes a thing properly belong to someone — and is the clash about ownership or about what counts as labour?
cases.rationale: Cross-tradition belonging-comparison forced to about-what question.

sonnet.answer:
**Manu's foundational claim:** The Brahmin is the primary recipient of dakṣiṇā (sacrificial fee/gift) not because he has produced anything economically, but because his birth from Brahma's mouth places him as lord of all creation from the outset. Manusmṛti 1.88 assigns 'giving and receiving alms' as the Brahmin's divinely ordained function; 1.93 states the Brahmin is 'by right the lord of this whole creation' by virtue of origin, seniority, and Vedic preservation; 1.94 explains the cosmological basis — the Brahmin was created to convey offerings to gods and ancestors; and 1.100–101 make the entitlement fully explicit: 'Whatever exists in the world is the property of the Brahmana; by his pre-eminence of birth, the Brahmana is indeed entitled to this all. The Brahmana eats but his own food…it is through the compassion of the Brahmana that other people enjoy.' Dakṣiṇā in Mn_11.3–11.6 flows from this cosmic station: a king must give jewels to Vedic Brahmins as sacrificial fee; givers earn heaven; the Brahmin deserves the gift by being what he is, not by producing anything.

**Comte's foundational claim:** In Traité de la propriété Vol. I, Ch. 5 (charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99), Comte grounds property entirely in labour and self-ownership: 'Labor is therefore the principle that gives birth to properties; almost all come from this source' (p-1db575). The argument runs: a man's faculties belong to him by the laws of his nature; the qualities that make things valuable are products of human industry; therefore whatever a man creates without taking from another is his (p-f76ed9). 'The most legitimate means of obtaining a property is to produce it, or to receive it, by the effect of a free transmission, from the hands of those who have produced it or received it from the producers' (p-cd7e40). Property is defined as utility created by industry — without productive labour, there is nothing to own.

**Where they clash and on what:** The two traditions collide directly on what justifies ownership, but the clash is simultaneously about ownership and about what counts as labour — the two cannot be cleanly separated.

On ownership: For Comte, title flows from productive activity; the claim to a thing is established by the act of giving it utility through one's effort. For Manu, title flows from cosmic birth-rank; the Brahmin's entitlement to dakṣiṇā is ontological — prior to any act. 'Whatever exists in the world is the property of the Brahmana' is not an earned claim but a primordial metaphysical fact.

On labour: Comte defines labour as the individual, voluntary expenditure of faculty that creates utility in things — a strictly productive and material standard. Manu implicitly counts the Brahmin's cosmic function (preserving the Veda, performing sacrifice, conveying offerings to gods and ancestors) as the 'service' that justifies reception of dakṣiṇā. But this is not labour in Comte's sense: it is not the production of material utility by a free agent; it is the enactment of a cosmological role assigned by birth. The Brahmin's ritual maintenance of the universe (sacrifice → fire-oblation → rain → food, Mn_3.76) is a kind of cosmic reproduction, not economic production.

The deeper asymmetry: Comte would say the Brahmin has produced nothing that grounds a property claim — Vedic recitation does not create the utility of the field the Brahmin receives. Manu would say Comte's framework is blind to the prior cosmological fact that all things already belong to the Brahmin by the terms of creation itself; labour-based claims of other varnas are merely permitted by Brahminic compassion (Mn_1.101), not independently valid. The clash is therefore primarily about the seat of ownership (productive act vs. cosmic birth-station), but embedded within that is an irreducible disagreement about what counts as a rightful 'contribution' — physical productive labour vs. ritual cosmic maintenance.

cases.prompt: Compare Manu chapter 7 on the king's qualifications with what Visnu Smriti says, then with what Brhaspati Smriti adds about the king's role in legal procedure. Three voices on kingship.
cases.rationale: Three-text rajadharma comparison.

sonnet.answer:
All three texts are present in the corpus and offer distinct but related perspectives on kingship.

**Manu Chapter 7** (work_slug: unknown-manusmrti-347b76, chapter 7): Manu opens with the king's divine composition — he is formed from extracted particles of eight gods: Indra, Wind, Yama, the Sun, Agni, Varuna, the Moon, and Kubera (Mn_7.4–5). This cosmic origin means the king surpasses all beings in splendor and must not be despised even as a child (Mn_7.5–8). The central qualifying concept is mastery of Daṇḍa (the rod of punishment), which Manu treats as Dharma itself (Mn_7.18). To wield it rightly, the king must be truthful, deliberate, wise, knowing of the three goals (dharma, wealth, desire), pure, true to his word, follow the sacred treatises, and have good assistants (Mn_7.26, 31). He must conquer his senses and avoid eighteen vices — ten born of desire (hunting, gambling, sleeping by day, slander, women, intoxication, song/dance/music, and aimless wandering) and eight born of anger (malice, violence, treachery, envy, calumny, misappropriation of property, and harshness of speech and punishment) (Mn_7.44–48). Manu's king is primarily a righteous wielder of punishment who protects all varnas and āśramas (Mn_7.35) — the theology of kingship dominates, while procedural court mechanics are absent.

**Viṣṇu Smṛti Chapter 3** (work_slug: unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8, chapter 3): Viṣṇu opens concisely — 'Now, the duties of a king (rājadharma)' — and moves immediately to practical administration rather than divine-origin theology. The king must protect the people (Vi_3.2) and uphold the varṇa/āśrama system (Vi_3.3). He manages a hierarchical administrative structure: village chiefs, superintendents of ten, a hundred, and a whole district (Vi_3.7–14), each redressing wrongs at their level. He appoints specialist officials by quality — pious men for pious duties, skilled for finance, brave for military, fierce for fierce deeds (Vi_3.17–20). Diplomatically, he uses conciliation, dissension, bribery, and force as appropriate (Vi_3.38). For legal disputes, Viṣṇu specifies that the king should personally examine cases with learned Brahmins, or alternatively appoint a Brahmin to examine them (Vi_3.72–73). Court assessors must be of good birth, impartial toward friend and foe, and uncorrupted by lust, anger, fear, or greed (Vi_3.74). Viṣṇu echoes Manu's daṇḍa language (Vi_3.95 ~ Mn_7.25) but adds a more humane note: 'The king who is happy in his subjects' happiness, and who is unhappy in their sorrow … is glorified in heaven after death' (Vi_3.98). Compared to Manu, Viṣṇu is more administrative and less theological, giving brief but direct guidelines on the king's participation in legal adjudication.

**Bṛhaspati Smṛti** (work_slug: unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070, chapter 1 — titled 'The Section on Legal Procedure'): Bṛhaspati shares the divine-origin narrative (from Soma, Agni, Sun, Wind, Indra, Lord of Wealth, Lord of Waters, and Yama — Brh_1,1.6–7) but pivots immediately to the institutional mechanics of legal procedure. He systematizes the king's role far beyond the other two:

1. *Jurisdiction and topics*: Legal disputes are of two kinds — arising from wealth (fourteen sub-types including lending, deposits, boundary disputes, theft, partition) and violence (four sub-types: two kinds of assault, homicide, adultery) (Brh_1,1.9–16).

2. *Four means of decision*: dharma, legal procedure, custom, and the king's edict — the last being the highest override, superseding custom (Brh_1,1.18–21). This formally positions the king as the apex authority in law.

3. *Court conduct*: The king, together with his ministers and family priest, must examine legal disputes according to the treatise on dharma, 'free from anger and greed, truthful and self-controlled' (Brh_1,1.22).

4. *Ten-limbed court structure*: The court is compared to a sacrifice (Brh_1,1.83). The king is the head; the prāḍvivāka (the chief judicial officer who asks questions and cross-examines) is the mouth; the assessors are the arms; the Smṛti the hands; the accountant and scribe the shanks (Brh_1,1.84–88). The king is 'the speaker and chief, the ruler; the assessor is the examiner of the case' (Brh_1,1.88).

5. *Four court types*: stationary (town/village), non-stationary (mobile), sealed (with a presiding officer), and directed — 'one attended by the king' (Brh_1,1.57–58). The king's personal presence defines the highest court.

6. *Delegation and accountability*: 'The legal procedure, though one, has been declared to be manifold by the wise; the decider of it is the king, or a Brahmin of great learning' (Brh_1,1.67). The king accompanied by the prāḍvivāka, ministers, priest, and assessors 'acts as an observer according to dharma' (Brh_1,1.70). He must protect both plaintiff and defendant (Brh_1,1.60) and must not punish the unpunishable or fail to punish the punishable (Brh_1,1.77).

**Synthesis — three voices**: Manu establishes the metaphysical ground (divine origin) and the moral-qualitative requirements for kingship, centering on the proper wielding of Daṇḍa. Viṣṇu translates this into administrative and governance duties, adding brief but clear directives about the king's personal role in adjudicating disputes with Brahmin assistance. Bṛhaspati elaborates legal procedure into a formal institutional system where the king sits at the apex as head of a ten-limbed court, surrounded by the prāḍvivāka, assessors, scribe, and accountant — transforming the king from sole wielder of punishment into the presiding head of a differentiated judicial apparatus. The progression is from theology (Manu) → governance (Viṣṇu) → procedural jurisprudence (Bṛhaspati).

cases.prompt: Compare the role of cleansing rituals (snana, achamana) in Manu chapter 5 and Yajnavalkya chapter 1.
cases.rationale: Tests precise cross-text comparison on purification rules.

sonnet.answer:
Both Manu chapter 5 (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, chapter 5 in corpus / Mn_5 verses) and Yajnavalkya chapter 1 (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6, chapter 1) treat snana (bathing) and achamana (ritual water-sipping) as foundational cleansing acts, but they frame, elaborate, and situate them differently.

**Achamana (ritual sipping):**
Manu 5.139 prescribes sipping water three times followed by wiping the mouth twice for purification, with women and Shudras sipping once. Manu 5.145 lists the occasions: after sleeping, sneezing, eating, spitting, speaking untruths, drinking water, and before study. The formulation is functional — a list of when to do it.

Yajnavalkya 1 is considerably more precise and ceremonial. Yj_1.18–21 specify: one must sit in a pure place with knees together, facing north or east, using the Brahma-tirtha (base of the thumb). Yj_1.20 adds that the water must be in its natural state, free from foam and bubbles. Yj_1.21 differentiates the three twice-born varnas by the depth of water ingested — reaching the heart (Brahmana), throat (Kshatriya), and palate (Vaishya) — while women and Shudras need only touch their lips once. Yj_1.19 names all four tirthas of the hand (Prajapati, Pitr, Brahma, Deva). Yj_1.106 frames achamana as what makes food 'immortal' by sipping before and after eating. Yj_1.196 repeats: after bathing, drinking, sneezing, sleeping, eating, or walking a public road, one must sip again even if already purified.

So on achamana: Yajnavalkya 1 supplies a richer procedural grammar (posture, hand position, direction, water quality, varna-differentiated depth); Manu 5 is comparatively spare (frequency only, varna distinction limited to once vs. three times).

**Snana (bathing):**
In Manu 5, snana appears almost exclusively within the long asauca (death-and-birth impurity) section (Mn_5.57–145). Mn_5.85 prescribes bathing after touching a Chandala, menstruating woman, newly-delivered woman, corpse, or one who touched a corpse. Mn_5.87 allows sipping water (not full bathing) as an alternative after touching a dry bone. Mn_5.103 prescribes bathing with clothes on after voluntarily following a corpse. Mn_5.144 requires bathing after sexual intercourse and after vomiting/purging. Mn_5.105 enumerates the purifying agents in a philosophical register: 'Knowledge, tapas, fire, food, earth, mind, water, plastering with cow-dung, wind, ritual action, the sun, and time.' Mn_5.109 offers: 'The limbs are purified by water; the mind is purified by truth; the individual soul, by sacred learning and tapas.' This metaphysical layering of purification (body vs. mind vs. soul) is unique to Manu 5.

In Yajnavalkya 1, snana is introduced early and positively as part of the student's daily regimen, not primarily as a response to death-pollution. Yj_1.22 distinguishes three modes of bathing: with mantras sacred to the water deities (mantra-snana), by sprinkling (prokshana), and through breath-control (pranayama), alongside sun worship and Gayatri recitation. Yj_1.100 locates bathing in the householder's morning routine — bathe, then perform libations to gods and ancestors. Yj_1.159 gives the canonical list of permitted bathing locations: rivers, god-made reservoirs, tanks, lakes, and springs; a parallel to Manu (Mn_4.203 in the adjoining corpus chunk) but with the added rule that one must remove five handfuls of clay before bathing in another's water.

**Key contrasts:**
1. *Contextual placement*: Manu 5 embeds snana/achamana inside the asauca system — these rituals are primarily activated by death and birth pollution. Yajnavalkya 1 introduces achamana within the student's foundational daily conduct (Yj_1.15–22) and snana within the householder's morning routine, treating them as affirmative daily disciplines rather than reactive decontamination.
2. *Procedural detail*: Yajnavalkya 1 is markedly more elaborate on achamana (tirthas, posture, direction, water quality, varna-differentiated depth). Manu 5 is sparser on the ritual mechanics.
3. *Typology of bathing*: Yajnavalkya 1 classifies bathing into at least three types (mantra-snana, sprinkling, pranayama-based), with snana explicitly joined to sun-worship and Gayatri. Manu 5 does not distinguish snana types.
4. *Philosophical framing*: Manu 5 uniquely situates water-purification within a broader ontology (Mn_5.105, 5.109), showing body-water-mind-soul as a ladder of purification, with moral and epistemic dimensions. Yajnavalkya 1 stays procedural.
5. *Gender and varna distinctions*: Both texts restrict full achamana to twice-born males and allow women/Shudras only a reduced form, but Yajnavalkya 1 elaborates the varna hierarchy within the twice-born group (heart/throat/palate) in a way Manu 5 does not.

cases.prompt: Which works in the corpus discuss the soul's journey after death?
cases.rationale: Tests broad discovery across philosophy of death. Should land Manusmriti ch. 12, Yajnavalkya ch. 3, San Hyan Kamahayanikan, perhaps Cynewulf's Juliana, Old English 'Soul and Body II'.

sonnet.answer:
Multiple works in the corpus discuss the soul's journey after death, spanning Old English Christian poetry, Sanskrit Dharmashastra, Kawi (Old Javanese) tantric and Buddhist texts, and Urdu poetry:

1. **Andreas** (cynewulf-andreas-07b573, ch. 1) — Old English poem. Souls of the righteous depart at death and seek 'the joys of the heavens, the home of the angels' (p-c655bd). Villains end 'in hell's jaw' (p-36b773). Angels snatch souls of the saved 'flying on the air, with clamorous bliss' (p-f115de). Heaven is the destination of the faithful after martyrdom.

2. **Āṅgirasa Smṛti** (unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1, ch. 1) — Sanskrit legal text. After death, sinners fall into named hells (Raurava, Kālasūtra, Aṅgāraśayana). Ancestors (pitrs) persist and must be satisfied through śrāddha rites. Yama, the lord of the dead, receives souls and chastises the wicked (p-e904f9, p-b50721).

3. **Manusmṛti** (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, ch. 6 and ch. 12) — Sanskrit Dharmashastra. Ch. 6 describes the ascetic contemplating 'the departure from this body, and the rebirth in a womb, and the passages of this inner soul through thousands of millions of wombs' (p-89d810). Ch. 12 gives an elaborate account: sinful souls receive torments from Yama in a body formed from the five elements; souls are then reborn according to their gunas — sattva souls ascend to gods, rajas to humans, tamas to beasts; great sinners cycle through hells and low-animal wombs; liberation (passing beyond the five elements) is the highest goal (p-074f6e, p-6dc12a).

4. **San Hyan Tattvajnana** (unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd, ch. 1) — Kawi Shaiva text. Provides a graduated account of the soul's post-death fate: the ātmā falls into hell, is judged by the host of Yama, and is reborn at a level determined by the grade of its evil karma — deformed human birth, triyak (animal) birth, or lower. The cycle of rebirth (samsara) is explicitly contrasted with liberation (p-8805a9, p-9b98fb, p-d53c24).

5. **Gaṇapatitattva** (unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136, ch. 1) — Kawi tantric text. Describes two paths the soul can take at the moment of death: 'Sacred True Transcendence' (liberation, kaivalya) versus 'Sacred False Transcendence' (rebirth/samsara). The soul's path at death depends on whether it holds the three-syllable mantra and achieves correct breath retention; the sacred Self then departs upward through the crown to merge with Shiva (p-21ebe8, p-0b45bd).

6. **Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana** (unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a, ch. 1) — Kawi Buddhist text. Explicitly asks 'what is the cause of rebirth for all in the world?' A dead sinner begs Yama's host for rebirth; the judge refuses, explaining that renewed life would only bring more destruction. The moksha-seeking ascetic desires 'freedom from rebirth' (p-e545f7, p-3677b7, p-690bb0).

7. **San Hyan Kamahayanikan** (unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19, ch. 1) — Kawi Mahayana Buddhist text. Mentions 'release from the wheel of existence, attainment of a happy rebirth, arrival at transcendent bliss' as outcomes of wholesome actions (p-dae921).

8. **San Hyan Mahajnana** (unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531, ch. 1) — Kawi Shaiva text. Teaches that those who achieve dissolution through correct practice 'will not be subject to rebirth'; the cycle of birth and suffering is called illusion (p-de9afc, p-6b88b2).

9. **Bang-E-Dara Part 3 — 'Sair-e-Falak'** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8, ch. 18) — Iqbal's Urdu poem of a celestial journey. The speaker ascends through the cosmic spheres, sees Paradise (with Houris and goblets), then perceives Hell as a frozen void where 'the souls from Earth who to this place are brought / bring their own embers, from the lives they wrought' (p-9d0a05, p-44c8f4). The afterlife is depicted as shaped by the soul's earthly deeds.

10. **Bang-E-Dara Part 1 — 'Mehr-e-roshan'** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c, ch. 32) — Iqbal's philosophical elegy. A meditation addressed to 'the sleepers in dust,' questioning the nature of the afterlife: 'Is Paradise a garden, or a stage of final rest? ... Is Hell a mechanism just to burn all sin away? ... What is this secret, which we men on earth call death's long night?' (p-f58ab2, p-12f695). Does not assert a specific eschatology but treats the soul's post-death existence as the central philosophical problem.

Works in the corpus that do NOT substantially discuss the soul's post-death journey: the Smritis (Brhaspati, Katyayana, Narada, Parasara, Yajnavalkya, Yama, Visnu) focus on legal rules and ritual procedure with only incidental references to hell/heaven as sanctions; the Old English Elegies focus on earthly loss and transience; the Urdu ghazals of Ghalib and Zauq use paradise/hell as metaphors for love rather than as eschatological doctrines; the French political-economy works (Dunoyer, Comte) only mention afterlife beliefs in passing anthropological descriptions; Cynewulf's Elene and Juliana touch on heaven as a destination but do not develop the soul's journey as a theme.

cases.prompt: Which texts in the corpus contain explicit cosmogonies (creation accounts)?
cases.rationale: Tests broad discovery. Manusmriti ch. 1 most explicit; Visnu Smriti has parallel material; tattva texts may also contain cosmogonic passages.

sonnet.answer:
Five texts in the corpus contain explicit cosmogonies (creation accounts):

1. **Manusmṛti** (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, chapter 1): The most complete cosmogony. Opens with the universe in primordial darkness, then the Self-Existent One manifests, casts his seed into the waters, from which a golden egg is born containing Brahma. The egg splits to form heaven and earth. Subsequent verses describe the creation of mind, ego, the five elements (ether, wind, fire, water, earth), time, the Vedas, gods, living beings, and the four varnas (Mn_1.5–1.41).

2. **Viṣṇu Smṛti** (unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8, chapter 1): Opens with Vishnu as the Cosmic Boar (Varāha) lifting the Earth from the primordial ocean after the night of Brahma. He distributes the waters, creates the seven netherworlds, seven upper worlds, continents, and oceans (Vi_1.1–1.15).

3. **Gaṇapatitattva** (unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136, chapter 1): Isvara explains to Ganapati how from Omkara emerges the bindu, from which arise the five deities (Brahma, Vishnu, Rudra, Isvara, Sadashiva), then the five subtle elements, then the gross cosmos (earth, water, sun/moon/stars, wind, voice, plants, animals) — paragraphs 2 and 4 (p-f03790, p-b07fcd).

4. **San Hyan Tattvajnana** (unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd, chapter 1): Contains an extended emanation cosmogony. Sadashivatattva creates all the gods and the world (earth, water, fire, wind, ether, moon, sun, constellations) through his Kriyasakti (paragraph 4, p-9fea48). A further sequence describes how the five tanmatras produce the five mahabhuttas, which are assembled by the Lord into the cosmic egg (andabhuvana) with seven upper and seven lower worlds (paragraphs 13–14, p-76faff, p-314d51).

5. **Vrhaspatitattva** (unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28, chapter 1): Sadashivatattva creates through Maya and its coarsening into trigunatattva (sattva, rajas, tamas), which in turn generates buddhi, ahamkara, the senses, tanmatras, and the five gross elements that constitute the world — an implicit cosmogonic sequence embedded in the tattva exposition (paragraphs around p-643885 onward).

No other works in the corpus (Old English elegies/poems, Urdu poetry collections, French political-economic treatises, Sanskrit dharmasastra smritis other than Manusmrti and Visnu Smrti, or the German Fichte pamphlet) contain explicit cosmogonies.

cases.prompt: Which works deal with the question of legitimate political authority?
cases.rationale: Tests cross-genre discovery. Manu ch. 7, Visnu rajadharma, Comte's whole project, Fichte's Zurückforderung, Iqbal's Tarana-e-Milli and Wataniyat. Cross-traditional sweep.

sonnet.answer:
Several works in the corpus deal directly with the question of legitimate political authority:

1. **Charles Comte, Traité de Législation: VOL I** (`charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2`): The most extensive treatment. Ch. 6 ('Of the system in which civil and political laws are considered as consequences of a primitive convention…') critically examines Rousseau's Social Contract as a theory of political legitimacy — asking what gives any convention over others the force of law. Ch. 7 ('Of the system in which laws are considered as the expression of the general will…') interrogates whether the 'general will' can coherently ground legislative authority. Ch. 13 ('Distinction between an arbitrary regime and a legal regime') directly asks what makes political rule legitimate rather than merely coercive.

2. **Johann Gottlieb Fichte, Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens** (`johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde`): Ch. 2 poses the question explicitly — 'dare to ask the prince who wishes to rule you, by what right he rules over you?' — and refutes hereditary right, divine right, and conquest as grounds for authority. Fichte argues the only legitimate political authority is a social contract in which each member voluntarily transfers alienable rights; the prince derives authority solely from this transfer by society.

3. **Manusmṛti** (`unknown-manusmrti-347b76`): Ch. 7 ('The Dharma of Kings') presents the Indian dharmaśāstra theory of legitimate royal authority — the king is composed of divine particles drawn from Indra, Agni, Yama, the Sun, and other gods, making him a 'great deity in human form' whose authority is cosmologically grounded.

4. **Bṛhaspati Smṛti** (`unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070`): Ch. 1, section 1.1 ('The Qualities of a King') likewise grounds royal authority in divine mandate — the king's form was created by extracting particles of energy from the gods, and beings submit to him 'out of fear' so that dharma is maintained; without a king, agriculture, trade, and protection would collapse.

In summary: Traité de Législation Vol I and Fichte's pamphlet engage the question from a liberal/Enlightenment contractarian standpoint (what social or rational grounds legitimate rule?), while the Manusmṛti and Bṛhaspati Smṛti engage it from the Sanskrit dharmaśāstra standpoint (divine mandate and cosmic order as the basis of kingship).

cases.prompt: Find every passage in the corpus that mentions wine or intoxication.
cases.rationale: Tests motif-search across diverse corpus. Will pull Ghalib, Zauq, Iqbal heavily. Maybe smriti's prayascittas for surapana.

sonnet.answer:
The corpus contains wine and intoxication mentions across 16 works. Here is a full breakdown by work:

**Diwan-E-Ghalib** (mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c): The most extensive coverage — wine, tavern (kharabaat), the Saaqi (wine-bearer), drunkenness, and intoxication appear across dozens of chapters including ch.2, 6, 7, 14, 20, 21, 35, 42, 50, 51, 58, 59, 61, 62, 68, 70, 75, 77, 78, 100, 105, 124, 128, 131, 132, 134, 138, 146, 150, 151, 155, 169, 176, 181, 188, 190, 194, 198, 202, 239. Wine here functions as a Sufi metaphor for divine love alongside literal references.

**Bang-E-Dara Part 1** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c): ch.4 ('wine kept in delicate crystal goblets'), ch.5 ('votary of wine'), ch.9 ('drinking wine is not a sin'), ch.17 ('Passion's wine'; 'drunk on wines of its own mind'), ch.18 ('wine of this brimming chalice'), ch.23 ('wine of knowledge'), ch.25 ('every bud is drunk with life'), ch.28 ('evening's hem is stained with wine'), ch.32 ('drunk on the wine of sleep'), ch.36 ('serve the wine of love'), ch.40 ('wine's effect'), ch.43 ('strange and silent tavern'), ch.44 ('pious wine was filled').

**Bang-E-Dara Part 2** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c): ch.3 ('trade of wine'), ch.4 ('O Wine, how you have loved your hidden state'), ch.5 ('world becomes one tavern, and each soul will be a drunken wight'), ch.8 ('vintage wine be so warm'), ch.11 ('sacred wine's celestial fate'), ch.14 ('drunk on the wine of their Fate'; 'Drunk on the wine of its own creation'), ch.16 ('tavern of morn'), ch.26 ('Love is the purest wine'), ch.28 ('creed of the wine-cup').

**Bang-E-Dara Part 3** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8): ch.4 ('New drinkers, and new wine'; 'drunk on wealth'), ch.7 ('drunk on wine that idle pleasure sings'), ch.11 ('drunk on the wine of devotion'), ch.18 (saaqi and wine references in transliteration), ch.20 ('drunk with their pride').

**Diwan-E-Zauq** (sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798): ch.4 (tavern), ch.15 ('tavern's wine domain'; 'water would turn into wine'), ch.38 ('wine of joy from heaven's tavern'), ch.45 ('give my rival the wine cup'), ch.56 ('tavern has no place'), ch.57 ('do not bolt the tavern door').

**Andreas** (cynewulf-andreas-07b573): ch.1 — 'hallowed wine from water' (miracle), 'wine-halls wasted', 'wine-city' (compound kenning), 'mead-pouring sorrow'.

**Old English Elegies** (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e): ch.1 (mead-hall), ch.2 ('mead-bench...ale-soaked warrior...wine-sated man...mead-mad man' — explicit intoxication causing death), ch.5 ('sipping of mead'), ch.6 ('mead-giving'), ch.7 ('mead-hall'), ch.8 ('mead-halls'; 'mead-joys'), ch.11 ('mead-hall'), ch.14 ('mead-hall'), ch.16 ('mead-hall full of human joys').

**Elene** (cynewulf-elene-d2d132): ch.1 — 'mead-hall he received treasures'.

**Manusmṛti** (unknown-manusmrti-347b76): ch.7 — 'intoxication' listed among the tenfold vices of kings ('gambling, sleeping by day, slander, women, intoxication, the triad of song, dance, and music, and aimless wandering').

**Parāśara Smṛti** (unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be): ch.10 — a woman 'overcome by intoxication or delusion'.

**Slokantara** (unknown-slokantara-d7a628): ch.1 — explicit discussion of the three causes of intoxication: sura (palm wine), learning/eloquence, and wealth (Lakṣmī).

**Vratisasana** (unknown-vratisasana-d0fe75): ch.1 — multiple passages: intoxication listed as a source of moral failure ('anger comes greed; from greed comes delusion; from delusion comes intoxication; from intoxication comes envy; from envy comes violence'), as a mitigating factor in vows, and as an aggravating factor in violations.

**Yājñavalkya Smṛti** (unknown-yajnavalkya-smtp-cb88d6): ch.2 — 'no punishment for acts done through intoxication, madness, and the like'.

**Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana** (unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a): ch.1 — 'doubt and intoxication' named as causes of a broken understanding.

**Nouveau traité d'économie VOL I** (charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce): ch.12 — workers spending wages at the tavern.

**Traité de Législation VOL I** (charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2): ch.18 — a man spending his income in a tavern, used as a moral/economic example.

cases.prompt: Which texts in the corpus speak directly to the reader in the second person?
cases.rationale: Cynewulf's epilogues; Iqbal's exhortations; Fichte addresses the princes. Tests rhetorical-mode search.

sonnet.answer:
Several texts in the corpus speak directly to the reader (or practitioner/addressee) in the second person:

1. **Fichte — Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit (both chapters)**: The most explicit and sustained second-person address to the reader. The author directly harangues 'you peoples' and 'you princes' throughout: 'No, you peoples, give up everything, everything, but not freedom of thought'; 'do not hate your princes; you should hate yourselves'; 'The times of barbarism are over, you peoples…' (ch. 2). (work_slug: johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde, chapters 1–2)

2. **Iqbal — Bang-E-Dara Part 3, ch. 13 (Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam / Address to the Muslim Youth)**: Explicitly titled 'An Address to the Muslim Youth' and sustains a direct second-person address throughout: 'Have you ever, O Muslim youth, paused for solemn reflection? / What was that firmament, of which you are but a shattered star?' (work_slug: allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8, chapter 13)

3. **Iqbal — Bang-E-Dara Part 3, ch. 4 (Dil se jo baat nikalti hai)**: Addresses the Muslim community directly in the second person: 'You are the ones who have no worldly art; / You are the folk who do not care to start / To build a nest…' (work_slug: allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8, chapter 4)

4. **Old English Elegies, ch. 15 (Wilt thu, fus haele)**: The narrator directly addresses the reader: 'Learn now this lore. I shall swiftly tell you / of the Measurer's might…' and 'Hear now this song of praise and make firm your thought.' (work_slug: unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e, chapter 15)

5. **Old English Elegies, ch. 6 (Maxims I)**: Opens with a direct second-person address: 'Question me with wise words! Do not let your spirit be hidden, / nor conceal what you know most deeply. / I will not tell you my secrets, if you hide from me your mind's craft.' (work_slug: unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e, chapter 6)

The following Kawi (Old Javanese) instructional/theological texts also speak directly to the disciple/practitioner in sustained second-person throughout their translations — the practitioner is the textual addressee:

6. **San Hyan Kamahayanikan** (ch. 1): 'you must know the ultimate secret'; 'Be at ease in your mind'; 'you are the body of a Divine Buddha this very instant'. (work_slug: unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19)

7. **San Hyan Mahajnana** (ch. 1): 'You must know this, O revered Kumāra'; 'you are the one, my son, Kumāra'. (work_slug: unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531)

8. **Vrhaspatitattva** (ch. 1): 'the kinds of tattva you must know'; 'Here are their characteristics, which I will explain to you'. (work_slug: unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28)

9. **Ganapatitattva** (ch. 1): 'My son, Gaṇapati, listen now to my teachings to you'; 'which you must know, my son, Gaṇapati'. (work_slug: unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136)

10. **Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana** (ch. 1): 'Be diligent in listening to my instruction, do not take it lightly. For now you will find the fruit of my words'. (work_slug: unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a)

11. **Vratisasana** (ch. 1): 'The mind of equanimity, that is what you must have as your mind, O viku'. (work_slug: unknown-vratisasana-d0fe75)

Note: Many Iqbal poems address non-reader entities (Sun, Candle, Sicily) in second person — these are apostrophes to subjects, not direct address to the reader. Similarly, Old English Elegies ch. 17 depicts a father addressing his son in second person, but that is dialogue within the narrative.

cases.prompt: Which works in the corpus mention specifically Greek or Roman antiquity?
cases.rationale: Comte's Vol IV explicitly; Iqbal nowhere directly in this corpus; possibly Cynewulf via biblical history.

sonnet.answer:
Ten works in the corpus specifically mention Greek or Roman antiquity:

1. **cynewulf-elene-d2d132** (Elene, ch. 1) — The poem is set in the Roman era and explicitly refers to Constantine as 'the Caesar commanded, helm of the Romans,' 'lord of the Romans,' and 'king of the Romans.'

2. **charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce** (Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I, chs 2, 4, 6, 7, 8, 11) — Mentions the Roman empire, Homer and 'the Greeks of Homer,' Aristotle, and Socrates.

3. **charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03** (Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II, chs 19) — Mentions Aristotle (Greek philosopher), Virgil and Horace (Roman poets).

4. **charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2** (Traité de Législation: VOL I, chs 1, 2, 9, 10, 11, 15, 17, 19, 22) — Mentions 'ancient peoples of Greece and Rome,' Aristotle, Plato (Greek), Cicero (Roman), Roman law, Roman empire, Roman republic.

5. **charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d** (Traité de Législation: VOL II, chs 2, 14) — Mentions 'ancient peoples of Italy and Greece' and Homer.

6. **charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a** (Traité de Législation: VOL III, chs 30, 36, 37, 42, 48) — Mentions ancient Greece, 'republics of Greece,' Roman Republic, Roman empire, Roman conquests, Homer, Cicero, Virgil, Horace.

7. **charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040** (Traité de Législation: VOL IV, chs 2–7, 13, 17, 23) — Mentions Roman empire, Roman Republic, Roman senators, Aristotle, Plato, Cicero, Caesar, Pompey, Homer, Virgil.

8. **charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99** (Traité de la propriété: VOL I, chs 1, 7, 9, 12–20) — Mentions Roman law extensively, Aristotle, Plato, Cicero, Roman Republic, Roman empire under Augustus.

9. **charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5** (Traité de la propriété: VOL II, chs 31, 44, 45, 47) — Mentions Roman empire, Aristotle, Virgil.

10. **sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798** (Diwan-E-Zauq, ch. 15) — References 'Platos' (the Greek philosopher Plato) in a ghazal.

All other works in the corpus (Iqbal's Bang-E-Dara, Ghalib's Diwan, Cynewulf's Andreas and Juliana, Old English Elegies, Fichte's Denkfreiheit, and all Sanskrit/Kawi/Indic texts) did not surface specific mentions of Greek or Roman antiquity in English-body searches.

cases.prompt: Which works in the corpus mention specifically Mughal-era figures or settings?
cases.rationale: Ghalib's diwan, Zauq's diwan (court poet), Iqbal's 'Goristan-e-Shahi'.

sonnet.answer:
Several works in the corpus mention Mughal-era figures or settings, primarily through Iqbal's Bang-e-Dara collections:

1. **Bang-e-Dara Part 3** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8):
   - Ch. 2 'Bilad-e-Islamiya': Most explicit Mughal-era setting. Directly names **Delhi** as a sacred site — 'The soil of Delhi is a shrine for the grief-stricken heart' — and refers to the Muslim sovereigns buried there: 'In this dust sleep the sovereigns of the Best of Nations, On whose governance the world's own order turned.' Also states 'If the nationhood of Islam were bound to any single place, Then its foundation would be India.' These sovereigns are the Mughal rulers whose graves are in Delhi.
   - Ch. 9 'Goristan-e-Shahi' (The Royal Cemetery): Mentions the **Koh-i-Noor** — 'The Koh-i-Noor has seen kings crowned and dead' — the famous Mughal crown jewel, in the context of a fallen royal nation. The poem mourns a fallen Muslim/Mughal civilization.

2. **Bang-e-Dara Part 1** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c):
   - Ch. 26 'Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet': References the Mughal dynasty obliquely — 'The land the Tatars for their homeland made' (the Mughals were of Timurid/Tatar descent) and 'Where for the Turks great diamond wealth was found' (Turks/Mughals and their jewel wealth).

3. **Diwan-e-Zauq** (sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798): The entire work is rooted in the Mughal court — Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq was the court poet of the last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar. The ghazal texts themselves (as translated) are classical love poetry and do not name Mughal rulers explicitly, but the work is intrinsically set in the late Mughal court context.

4. **Vīramitrodaya** (unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632): Written during the Mughal era by Mitra Miśra; its patronage prologue praises King Vīrasiṃha (Vira Singh Deva Bundela), a Mughal-era noble who was a vassal of Emperor Jahangir. The text does not name any Mughal emperor explicitly, but the Bundela king praised is a recognizable Mughal-period figure.

No other works in the corpus (Old English poems, Sanskrit Smritis, Kawi texts, French political economy works, or the German Fichte text) contain Mughal-era figures or settings based on my searches.

cases.prompt: Which works in the corpus discuss the nature of legitimate evidence in a legal dispute?
cases.rationale: Yajnavalkya ch. 2 (sakshya), Brhaspati Smriti (lekhya), Naradasmriti, Manu ch. 8.

sonnet.answer:
Several works in the corpus discuss the nature of legitimate evidence in a legal dispute, all from the Sanskrit Dharmaśāstra (legal/jurisprudential) tradition:

1. **Kātyāyana Smṛti** (unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2) — the most detailed treatment. Chapter 24 is explicitly titled 'Means of Proof, and Deliberation on their Relative Strength' and defines proof as twofold: divine (ordeals) and human (document, witnesses, possession). It discusses which form of evidence is stronger, when each applies, and how a litigant who abandons a stronger proof loses the right to revert to it. Chapters 25 and 27 continue with documents and possession respectively.

2. **Bṛhaspati Smṛti** (unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070) — Chapter 1 covers the relative strength of means of proof (document, witnesses, possession, divine ordeal), who bears the burden of proof (plaintiff vs. defendant), and when the king himself serves as the last resort means of proof when no other evidence exists.

3. **Nāradasmṛti** (unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2) — Chapter 1 enumerates the three kinds of evidence (written document, witnesses, possession) and discusses sureties and pledges as instruments of proof.

4. **Manusmṛti** (unknown-manusmrti-347b76) — Chapter 8 discusses what kinds of witnesses creditors should appoint in legal proceedings and how they must speak the truth, as well as rules of legal proceedings more broadly.

5. **Yājñavalkya Smṛti** (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6) — Chapter 2 touches on legal proceedings and invalid acts in evidence.

6. **Yama Smṛti** (unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d) — Chapter 1 specifies categories of persons whose evidence is not valid (associates, the very young and old, those oppressed by the king).

cases.prompt: Which works in the corpus mention specific Indian rivers?
cases.rationale: Iqbal's 'Kinaar-e-Ravi' (Ravi); Manu's discussions of Saraswati region; possibly others.

sonnet.answer:
Four works in the corpus mention specific Indian rivers:

1. **Manusmṛti** (`unknown-manusmrti-347b76`): Chapter 2 names the Sarasvatī and Dṛṣadvatī rivers as the boundaries of Brahmavarta (Mn_2.17), and mentions Prayāga (Mn_2.21). Chapter 8 names the Ganga — 'do not go to the Ganga, nor to the Kurus' (Mn_8.92). Chapter 11 names the Sarasvatī again in a penance context (Mn_11.77).

2. **Viṣṇu Smṛti** (`unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8`): Chapter 85 contains the most extensive list, enumerating sacred bathing places including the Narmadā (Vi_85.8), Yamunā (Vi_85.9), Ganges (Vi_85.10), Mahāgaṅgā (Vi_85.23), Sarasvatī (Vi_85.27), Gaṅgādvāra (Vi_85.28), Kauśikī (Vi_85.35), Sarayū (Vi_85.36), Śoṇa and Jyotiṣā confluence (Vi_85.37), Godāvarī (Vi_85.46), Gomatī (Vi_85.47), Vetravatī (Vi_85.48), Vipāśā (Vi_85.49), Vitastā (Vi_85.50), Śatadrū (Vi_85.51), Candrabhāgā (Vi_85.52), Irāvatī (Vi_85.53), and Sindhu (Vi_85.54).

3. **Parāśara Smṛti** (`unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be`): Chapter 12 mentions the Gaṅgā — 'Prabhāsa and other pilgrimage sites, and the Gaṅgā and other rivers, all reside in a Brāhmaṇa's right ear' (Par_12.20).

4. **Bang-E-Dara Part 1** (`allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c`): Chapter 28 ('Kinaar-e-Ravi', meaning 'Banks of the Ravi') is an entire poem set on the Ravi River — 'The Ravi, lost in song, in evening's silence deep' (p-70a0dc).

cases.prompt: Which works in the corpus engage with the question of the Brahmin's privileges or duties?
cases.rationale: Manu, Yajnavalkya, Visnu, Brhaspati, Naradasmriti, Parasara. The full smriti corpus minus the Old Javanese tattvas.

sonnet.answer:
Multiple works in the corpus engage directly with Brahmin privileges and duties, all of them Dharmaśāstra (smṛti) texts in Sanskrit:

1. **Manusmṛti** (`unknown-manusmrti-347b76`) — the most extensive treatment. Chapter 1 lists Brahmin duties: 'To the Brahmanas he assigned teaching and studying, sacrificing for oneself and sacrificing for others, giving and receiving alms' (Mn_1.88), and asserts sweeping privileges: 'Whatever exists in the world is the property of the Brahmana; by his pre-eminence of birth, the Brahmana is indeed entitled to this all' (Mn_1.100). Chapter 10 adds: 'A Brahmana alone should teach them, not the other two' (Mn_10.1) and 'the Brahmana is the lord of the varnas' (Mn_10.3).

2. **Āṅgirasa Smṛti** (`unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1`) — Chapter 1 contains a dedicated section titled 'The Brahmin's Right to the Veda,' asserting that a Brahmin alone has the right to recite the Veda (Ang_1.159–160), discusses markers of Brahminhood (top-knot and sacred thread, Ang_1.62–65), and treats the duties of an ascetic 'with the permission of Brahmins' (Ang_1.143–146).

3. **Viṣṇu Smṛti** (`unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8`) — Chapter 2 opens with the four varṇas and explicitly states 'The duty of a Brāhmaṇa is teaching' (Vi_2.5), 'For the twice-born, sacrificing for others and studying the Veda are also duties' (Vi_2.9), and 'For a Brāhmaṇa, officiating at sacrifices for others and accepting gifts' as their prescribed livelihood (Vi_2.11).

4. **Parāśara Smṛti** (`unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be`) — Chapter 1 is titled 'The Conduct of the Four Social Classes' (cāturvarṇyācāra); Chapter 2 opens 'A Brāhmaṇa, while engaged in his six duties...' and discusses restrictions on Brahmin agriculture and the proper occupations for a Brahmin.

5. **Yājñavalkya Smṛti** (`unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6`) — Chapter 1 identifies the four varṇas and declares Brahmins (with Kṣatriyas and Vaiśyas) as the twice-born for whom sacramental rites from conception to cremation apply, and discusses student celibacy and conduct largely in the Brahmin frame.

The Kātyāyana Smṛti (`unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2`) also refers to a king being 'devoted to Brahmins' (K_001), treating Brahmin honor as a royal duty. The Bṛhaspati Smṛti and Nārada Smṛti focus primarily on legal procedure and do not foreground Brahmin privileges as a distinct topic.

cases.prompt: Which works in the corpus offer doctrines of selfhood (atman, khudi, soul, the I)?
cases.rationale: Iqbal's khudi project; Manu, Yajnavalkya on atman; San Hyan Tattvajnana, Vrhaspatitattva.

sonnet.answer:
Several works in the corpus offer explicit or sustained doctrines of selfhood:

1. **Gaṇapatitattva** (`unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136`, ch.1) — The most systematic self-doctrine in the corpus. It enumerates "The Five Selves": the Self, the Supreme Self, the Inner Self, the Non-Self, and the Void-Self. It identifies the Self as "Self-born and by itself worshipped" and as the true deity for sages, while the external liṅga is merely a symbol.

2. **San Hyan Tattvajnana** (`unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd`, ch.1) — A Kawi philosophical text with a detailed doctrine of the Divine Ātmā. It classifies Ātmikatattva (Self-principle) as one of three divisions of Śivatattva, and traces the ātmā's states (waking, dreaming, deep sleep, turīya), transmigrations driven by triguṇa, and eventual liberation (mokṣa).

3. **Vrhaspatitattva** (`unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28`, ch.1) — Another Kawi text with an extended doctrine of the ātmā as the lord who uses the ten sense organs as instruments. It discusses ātmā viparīta (distorted/inverted self), the soul's attachment to sensual enjoyment, and the path to liberation.

4. **Yājñavalkya Smṛti** (`unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6`, ch.3) — Provides a Sanskrit Vedāntic doctrine: individual souls emerge from the Supreme Self like sparks from iron (Yj_3.67); the practitioner must "see the subtle Self situated in himself through the yoga of meditation" (Yj_3.64); and the Puruṣa becomes individual soul through delusion, desire, aversion, and karma (Yj_3.125).

5. **Allama Iqbal — Bang-E-Dara Parts 1, 2, and 3** (`allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c`, `allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c`, `allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8`) — Iqbal's philosophy of khudi (selfhood/ego) runs throughout all three parts. Part 1 ch.9 urges seeking one's own true path rather than blind imitation; Part 2 ch.23 ("Swami Ram Tirath") refers to shattering the idol of the Self through Love. The collection as a whole articulates individual selfhood as the foundation of spiritual and civilizational renewal.

6. **San Hyan Mahajnana** (`unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531`, ch.1) — A Kawi text that addresses the body of the ātmā (the Self) as the dwelling place of the divine Lord; it poses questions about what sleeps, wakes, and wanders within the body, elaborating a doctrine of the self in relation to the divine.

7. **Nyaya Tilakam Pandulipi** (`unknown-nyaya-tilakam-pandulipi-bookny`, ch.5) — Within the Nyaya ontological framework, Self is listed as one of nine substances. It distinguishes atomic individual selves from the all-pervading Self of Ishvara (the supreme being), providing a metaphysical doctrine of selfhood.

8. **Manusmṛti** (`unknown-manusmrti-347b76`, ch.1) — Contains a cosmological doctrine in which the divine Self-existent One manifests the universe; from himself he produces ego (ahaṃkāra) and the great soul; "He who is... the soul of all beings" (Mn_1.7). This frames a doctrine of the universal self as cosmological ground.

The Urdu ghazal works (Diwan-E-Ghalib, Diwan-E-Zauq) engage self-related themes through be-khudi (ecstatic self-loss) but offer mystical poetics rather than systematic doctrines.

cases.prompt: Find every work in the corpus that addresses the question of moral action's reward (karma, recompense).
cases.rationale: Manu ch. 12, Yajnavalkya ch. 3, Visnu, Old English 'Soul and Body II', possibly Cynewulf's epilogues.

sonnet.answer:
Multiple works in the corpus address the question of moral action's reward (karma, recompense), spanning Sanskrit Dharmaśāstra texts, Kawi (Old Javanese) philosophical and narrative texts, and Old English Christian poetry.

**Most explicitly:**

1. **Manusmṛti** (unknown-manusmrti-347b76) — Chapter 12 is the corpus's most explicit treatment of karma. It opens with sages asking about 'the supreme fulfillment of the fruit of actions,' and Bhṛgu explains: 'Action, which springs from the mind, speech, and body, has good and bad results; the states of men, high, low, and middle, are born of their karma.' (Mn_12.3). The chapter systematically maps the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) to specific rebirths — from gods (sāttvic) to beasts (tāmasic). Chapter 11 (also read as part of this work) specifies penances/consequences for dozens of particular sins.

2. **Vrhaspatitattva** (unknown-vrhaspatitattva-b60b28) — Explicitly discusses karmavāsanā (karmic latency) and recompense: 'The truth of the fruit of karma is indeed endowed with existence, both here and in the hereafter' (p-635a93). It explains that good karma leads to heaven, evil karma leads to hell, and after the fruit is exhausted, one is reborn at the appropriate level.

3. **Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana** (unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a) — A Buddhist Kawi narrative in which the rakṣasa Kuñjarakarṇa visits Yama's realm and sees how moral actions determine destiny. Key passage: 'It is impossible not to experience the fruit of one's good and evil deeds. As for happiness and suffering, there is no other source for them. It is from one's own self that they come, following all past karma' (p-50854d). Also discusses three kinds of merit (giving, virtue, contemplation) and their fruits.

4. **Slokantara** (unknown-slokantara-d7a628) — Teaches that meritorious giving yields multiplied divine reward: on ordinary days one returns as 10; during eclipses, 100; at Kanyāgata, 1000; at the end of a yuga, countless (Slo_02). The quality of the recipient also multiplies the reward (Slo_03).

5. **Viṣṇu Smṛti** (unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8) — Chapter 43 catalogs 22 named hells and their inhabitants ('those who have committed heinous sins without performing prāyaścitta are tormented for a Kalpa'); Chapter 44 lists specific sins and their animal-rebirth consequences (e.g., grain-thief → rat; thief of water → water-bird).

**Also addressing moral recompense as a significant theme:**

6. **Yama Smṛti** (unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d) — Attributed to Yama, the god of moral judgment; prescribes specific penances for specific sins, framing moral action as entailing unavoidable consequences requiring remediation.

7. **Yājñavalkya Smṛti** (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6) — Chapter 3 (On Expiation / prāyaścittādhyāya) systematically addresses penances for moral violations, presupposing that immoral acts carry consequences that must be worked off.

8. **Parāśara Smṛti** (unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be) — Dharmaśāstra for the Kali Yuga addressing dharma, sin, and the duties that ensure moral recompense.

9. **Āṅgirasa Smṛti** (unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1) — Discusses prāyaścitta as the prescribed remedy for moral violations; Vyāhṛtis are declared 'the appeaser of all sins.'

10. **Old English Elegies** (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e) — Two elegies address moral recompense in a Christian frame: (a) **Soul and Body II** (chapter 10) states that 'the spirit takes from God Himself either torment or glory, as that earth-vessel had earned for it before in the world'; the soul rebukes the body on Doomsday for its sins. (b) **Vainglory** (chapter 12) contrasts the fate of the proud/sinful (bound for hell-torments) with the humble (ascending to glory), concluding: 'their rewards are not alike with the Glory-King.'

Works in the corpus that do NOT substantially address moral action's reward include the French political/legal texts (Dunoyer, Charles Comte), the German Fichte pamphlet, the Urdu poetry collections (Iqbal, Ghalib, Zauq), Bṛhaspati Smṛti (legal procedure), Nāradasmṛti (legal procedure), Kātyāyana Smṛti (legal/administrative), and the Cynewulf hagiographic poems (divine reward is implicit backdrop, not the systematic subject).

cases.prompt: Find every work in the corpus that opens by addressing or invoking the audience.
cases.rationale: Cynewulf's Andreas opening; Iqbal's anthems; Fichte's Zurückforderung.

sonnet.answer:
The following works in the corpus open by addressing or invoking the audience:

1. **Andreas** (cynewulf-andreas-07b573, ch. 1, p-868413): Opens with "We have heard of heroes in ages past, / of twelve true thanes under the turning stars" — the Old English bardic 'wē gehȳrdon' (we have heard) formula invites the listener into a shared recollection, a conventional Anglo-Saxon audience invocation.

2. **Juliana** (cynewulf-juliana-9a2157, ch. 1, p-529b94): Opens with "Hark! We have heard it, a history for heroes" — 'Hark!' is a direct call to the audience's attention; this is the clearest audience-invocation opening in the Old English works.

3. **Kalpabuddha** (unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc, ch. 1, p-e1c8df): Opens with "This is the royal command of the Lord, the divine Buddha, this great instruction must be heeded, listen to all the mantras" — 'must be heeded' and 'listen' are direct imperatives addressed to the reader/audience.

4. **San Hyan Kamahayanikan** (unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19, ch. 1, p-6eb5c0): Opens with "Come, child, I shall teach you the Great Vehicle, the method of the Path of the Master of Mantras. You are a worthy vessel for the Great Path." — explicit second-person address to the student/reader.

5. **San Hyan Tattvajnana** (unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd, ch. 1, p-d53c24): Opens with "Now, this must be heeded by the practitioner of the sacred duty who wishes to be released from the cycle of rebirth... That is what you must first understand" — the second-person 'you must' is a direct instruction to the audience.

Within the **Old English Elegies** collection (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e), which is catalogued as one work with 17 chapters (each a distinct poem), the first chapter does not address the audience, but four individual poems open with audience address:
- Ch. 6, Maxims I (p-84cf09): "Question me with wise words! Do not let your spirit be hidden" — explicit second-person address.
- Ch. 8, The Husband's Message (p-58bec2): "Now I specially shall speak to you" — direct 'you'.
- Ch. 12, Vainglory (p-946b37): "Listen, a wise sage told me in days of yore" — 'Listen' invokes the audience.
- Ch. 15 (p-583181): "Will you, eager hero, greet a curious man" — explicit second-person question to the audience.

Borderline: **Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit** (Fichte, johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde) has a Preface (ch. 1) that does not address the audience directly, but its main Speech (ch. 2, p-6e07a5) opens with "The times of barbarism are over, you peoples" — sustained direct address. If the Speech itself counts as the work's opening, Fichte belongs on this list.

cases.prompt: Which Old English elegies in the corpus speak of a man-and-his-lord relation?
cases.rationale: 'The Wanderer', 'Deor', 'The Wife's Lament', 'The Husband's Message'.

sonnet.answer:
Three elegies in the 'Old English Elegies' collection (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e) directly and substantively speak of the man-and-his-lord relation:

1. **The Wanderer (chapter 11)** — The most central treatment of the comitatus bond. The wanderer has lost his 'gold-friend' (lord) and wanders the ice-cold sea seeking a new treasure-giver. He recalls how his lord welcomed him to the feast and the gift-throne, and dreams of clasping and kissing 'his liege-lord.' The poem's entire sorrow flows from the rupture of the thane-lord bond.

2. **Deor (chapter 13)** — The closing stanza explicitly frames the poem as a scop's lament over losing his lord's favor: 'for a time I was the Heodenings' scop, dear to my lord… I had a goodly following, a loyal lord, until Heorrenda… received the land-right which the protector of earls had granted me before.' The man-and-lord bond, and its loss, is the poem's personal climax.

3. **Widsith (chapter 14)** — A wandering scop who 'followed many lords' across peoples and tribes. He describes his bond with his protecting lord Eadgils of the Myrgings (giving him the ring-gift of Eormanric as tribute) and the reciprocal exchange of song for ring-giving that structures every lord relationship in the poem.

Other elegies in the collection (e.g., The Seafarer, ch. 5; The Gifts of Men, ch. 1; The Fortunes of Men, ch. 2) mention the lord-thane relationship in passing, but it is not their primary elegiac concern.

cases.prompt: Which Iqbal poems in the corpus refer to specific historical Muslim victories or defeats?
cases.rationale: 'Shikwa' references Muslim conquests; 'Saqaliya' (Sicily); 'Bilad-e-Islamiya' (Muslim cities); 'Tarana-e-Milli'.

sonnet.answer:
Several Iqbal poems in the corpus (all from Bang-E-Dara Parts 1–3) refer to specific historical Muslim victories or defeats:

1. **Saqaliya** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c, ch. 21) — the clearest poem of historical defeat. It mourns the fall of Muslim Sicily ('the grave of Hejazi civilization'), explicitly laments the fall of Granada ('when heaven laid to waste the splendor of Granada, / A cry of anguish rose from the grieving heart of Ibn Badrun'), and alludes to the fall of Baghdad ('The nightingale of Shiraz raised its lament for Baghdad').

2. **Shikwa** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8, ch. 20) — invokes multiple specific historical victories: 'who was it that tore out Khyber's mighty door?' (Battle of Khaybar, 628 CE); 'Who was it that laid Caesar's city flat upon the floor?' (Byzantine conquests); 'Who was it that extinguished Zoroaster's sacred fire?' (defeat of the Sassanid Persian Empire); 'We called the prayer in Europe's churches' and 'in the Sea of Darkness, rode our horses, wild and free' (Muslim conquest of Spain/Al-Andalus, 711 CE).

3. **Bilad-e-Islamiya** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8, ch. 2) — references the fall of Cordoba ('Its flame went out, and scattered the Shining Nation's assembly'), the fall of Baghdad, and the conquest of Constantinople ('Now an eternal symbol of the Ummah's power and grandeur').

4. **Tarana-e-Milli** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8, ch. 23) — laments the loss of Andalusia: 'O Garden of Andalusia, do you recall the day, / When on your branches high our nest in safety lay?' (referencing the Reconquista/fall of Muslim Spain).

5. **Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8, ch. 13) — references the early Muslim victory over Persia: 'the nation which once crushed underfoot the crown upon the head of Darius'.

cases.prompt: Which works in the corpus address the question of how language defines a community?
cases.rationale: Iqbal's 'Larkiyan Parh Rahi Hain Angrezi' (English-language education critique); 'Tarana-e-Milli'; possibly Comte on national language.

sonnet.answer:
Several works in the corpus address how language defines a community, from multiple angles:

1. **Charles Dunoyer, "Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I" (charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce), Chapter 11** — The most explicit treatment. Dunoyer argues that 'a more particular community of language' and 'a greater conformity of customs' are what hold together the members of the same national aggregation (p-d284b3). He also observes that peoples are 'less separated still by distances than by language and customs' (p-e3770f), and predicts languages will eventually 'melt into a common idiom for all cultivated peoples.'

2. **Charles Comte, "Traité de la propriété: VOL I" (charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99), Chapter 7** — Comte argues that 'a community of sentiments, ideas, language, laws, and the habit of trading together, can merge, in a way, into a single nation populations that once formed as many separate peoples' (p-999f08). He further notes that some populations remain distinct by preserving 'a distinct idiom' even under the same government.

3. **Nyaya Tilakam Pandulipi (unknown-nyaya-tilakam-pandulipi-bookny), Chapter 16** — Addresses the question from a philosophical angle: 'conventional śabda-artha relationships established through community usage and verified through successful communication' (p-6c569b). This argues that language meaning is constituted by community practice — the community defines language conventions, and those conventions bind the community.

4. **Old English Elegies (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e), Chapter 6 (Maxims I)** — States that God 'gives us thought, diverse moods, many tongues... just as many peoples as there are customs' (p-84cf09), linking linguistic plurality to communal plurality — different languages correspond to different peoples.

5. **Allama Iqbal, "Bang-E-Dara Part 2" (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c), Chapter 2** — Iqbal takes a dissenting view: 'The foundation of our Community's fortress is not the unity of homeland' (p-25e981), arguing the Muslim Ummah is defined by faith, not by language or territory.

6. **Allama Iqbal, "Bang-E-Dara Part 3" (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8), Chapter 25 (Wataniyat) and Chapter 27** — Iqbal critiques territorial/linguistic nationalism as an 'idol' that destroys Islamic brotherhood. Chapter 27 satirizes adoption of English as the 'path to salvation,' implying that language shifts threaten community identity.

cases.prompt: Which works in the corpus contain ritual prescriptions for purification?
cases.rationale: Manu ch. 5, Visnu's chapters in the asauca cluster, Yajnavalkya ch. 1, Yama Smriti.

sonnet.answer:
Multiple works in the corpus contain ritual prescriptions for purification, almost all of them Sanskrit Dharmaśāstra (Smṛti) texts and related commentaries:

1. **Āṅgirasa Smṛti** (unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1, ch. 1) — The most extensive treatment: prescriptions for ritual impurity from birth (sūtaka) and death (āśauca/mṛtāśauca), specific penances (prāyaścitta) for transgressions (e.g., Prājāpatya, Taptakṛcchra, Cāndrāyaṇa penances), and rules declaring bathing as the supreme purifier in all impurity situations.

2. **Manusmṛti** (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, chs. 1, 2, 4, 6, 12) — Prescribes purifying saṃskāras from conception onward (ch. 2), impurity rules from birth and death and how to become pure by touching water (ch. 6), purifying objects at śrāddha rites (ch. 4), and fire-oblations and mantras for purifying hidden sins (ch. 12).

3. **Parāśara Smṛti** (unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be, chs. 1, 3, 4, 7, 8, 9) — Includes a dedicated Section on Penance (Prāyaścittakāṇḍam, ch. 4), a section explicitly titled 'Purification of Things' (dravyaśuddhiḥ, ch. 7), sūtaka rules for birth and death (ch. 3), and expiation rules for harming cattle (ch. 9).

4. **Viṣṇu Smṛti** (unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8, chs. 19–22) — Prescribes bathing with sesame paste at the end of the āśauca period (ch. 19), penance after eating during another's āśauca (ch. 22), and specific lengths of impurity by varṇa.

5. **Yājñavalkya Smṛti** (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6, ch. 1) — Contains a section explicitly on 'Purification of Things' (dravyaśuddhiprakaraṇam), prescribing purification procedures for gold, silver, conch shells, water vessels, and sacrificial cups.

6. **Vīramitrodaya** (unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632, ch. 1) — Prescribes ablutions with a vow (taking sandalwood paste on a tablet) and discusses śuddhi (purity) in ritual contexts.

7. **Slokantara** (unknown-slokantara-d7a628, ch. 1) — Briefly prescribes śuddhi as morning bathing to purify the body, alongside sun-worship and prayer.

8. **Kātyāyana Smṛti** (unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2, ch. 64) — Mentions sacred and purifying bathing places and rules about polluting them.

The Old English, French, German, and Urdu works in the corpus do not contain ritual purification prescriptions. The Kawi works do not surface explicit purification prescriptions in translation.

cases.prompt: Which works in the corpus discuss the rules for offering hospitality to a guest?
cases.rationale: Manu, Visnu, Yajnavalkya — atithi-dharma. Tests targeted ritual-rule discovery.

sonnet.answer: Several works in the corpus discuss rules for offering hospitality to a guest (atithi). The most detailed treatment is in the Manusmṛti (unknown-manusmrti-347b76), chapters 3 and 4, which prescribes: honoring guests is the 'sacrifice to men' (one of the five great daily sacrifices); a householder must give an arriving atithi a seat and water; guests should be fed before the householder eats; a guest who arrives at sunset must not be turned away; and failing to honor guests brings sin. The Parāśara Smṛti (unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be), chapter 1, similarly mandates hospitality to the atithi alongside the Vaiśvadeva rite, and warns that those who exclude the atithi from hospitality go to hell. The Yājñavalkya Smṛti (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6), chapter 1, lists 'hospitality to guests' as one of the mahāyajñas (great sacrifices) incumbent on the householder. The Āṅgirasa Smṛti (unknown-angirasa-smtp-2bf9d1), chapter 1, also mentions the worship of the guest (atithi) as a ritual duty and instructs that food should be accepted and given to one's guru, fire, and guests. Outside the Sanskrit Smritis, Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana (unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a, chapter 1) portrays a virtuous couple whose 'faith in guests was great,' illustrating hospitality as a dharmic virtue in the Kawi tradition.

cases.prompt: Which works in the corpus contain reflection on what it is to be young?
cases.rationale: Iqbal's 'Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam', 'Ehd-e-Tifli', children's poems; Manu's brahmacari rules.

sonnet.answer:
Several works in the corpus contain sustained reflection on what it is to be young:

1. **Old English Elegies** (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e): Multiple chapters engage this theme. Chapter 3 (The Wife's Lament) states: "Ever must a young man be grim of spirit, his heart's thoughts heavy; so too must he hold a cheerful face, though his heart is grieving, an endless sorrow" — framing youth as a time of forced stoic endurance. Chapter 6 (Maxims I) treats youth as a formative stage requiring guidance: "One should teach a young man, strengthen and instruct him that he may know well... One should not condemn him in his childhood, before he can prove himself." Chapter 11 (The Wanderer) reflects that "a man cannot become wise, before he has his share of winters in this world-kingdom," lamenting the fall of "the proud young thanes" to show youth as a state before wisdom.

2. **Bang-E-Dara Part 1** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c): Chapter 5 reflects: "While youth remains, there's joy in sight and pleasure in desire; / Our home's brief joy is but a guest's short stay, and all in vain" — framing youth as the window of desire and transient joy.

3. **Bang-E-Dara Part 2** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c): Chapter 11 ("Hah" / "The Joy of Today") is the most sustained meditation on being young in the corpus: "Ah, Youth! How long can it in hopeful longing burn? That joy is no true joy for which we wait and yearn... For Youth's one creed is 'The Joy of Today,' beyond all strife." It argues that Paradise's deferred rewards are alien to the soul of youth, which lives entirely in the present.

4. **Bang-E-Dara Part 3** (allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8): Chapter 7 ("Falsafa-e-Gham") calls youth "pleasant dreams" from which sorrow awakens the soul. Chapter 13 ("Address to the Muslim Youth") directly addresses young Muslims to reflect on their inheritance and potential. Chapter 22 ("Taleem aur iske Nataaij") laments the spiritual cost of the youth's modern education.

5. **Diwan-E-Ghalib** (mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c): Chapter 35 mourns the loss of youth — "That fire is missing, and the youth you had before is gone" — and chapter 95 reflects on how youth shapes expectation in love.

The richest reflections on the inner experience of being young come from the Old English Elegies (especially chapters 3, 6, and 11) and from Iqbal's Bang-E-Dara trilogy, especially Part 2 chapter 11.

cases.prompt: What does 'dharma' mean across the Sanskrit smriti tradition? Synthesise from at least three texts.
cases.rationale: Tests cross-text concept synthesis. Manu, Yajnavalkya, Visnu give different shadings.

sonnet.answer:
Across the Sanskrit smriti tradition, 'dharma' carries a cluster of overlapping meanings — sacred law, cosmic order, righteousness, duty, and right conduct — whose precise emphasis shifts by text, social context, and cosmic age.

1. Manusmṛti (unknown-manusmrti-347b76): Manu provides the most systematic treatment. In Mn_2.6 [p-d70b97] he declares: 'The entire Veda is the root-source of Dharma (the sacred law and eternal duty), as are Smṛti (the remembered tradition) and the conduct of those who know it, the practices of the virtuous, and one's own self-satisfaction.' This four-source definition (Veda, Smṛti, conduct of the good, self-satisfaction) frames dharma as simultaneously revealed law and internalised virtue. Mn_2.1 [p-c52281] adds a moral-intuitive dimension: 'That which is practiced by the learned and the good, who are ever free from hatred and passion, and which is assented to by the heart — know that to be Dharma.' Cosmologically, Mn_1.26 [p-84ef7f] presents dharma as a metaphysical principle: the Creator 'separated Dharma (righteousness) from non-Dharma' at creation, installing it as a cosmic polarity. Mn_1.81-82 [p-fcafde, p-1365cd] track dharma's progressive diminution across the four yugas ('Dharma is four-footed and complete' in the Krita age; in subsequent ages it loses a foot each time through theft, falsehood, and deceit). The text also treats dharma as varna-specific duty: the Brahmana's birth is 'an eternal incarnation of Dharma' (Mn_1.98, [p-307a84]), and each varna has assigned dharmas (Mn_1.88-91).

2. Yājñavalkya Smṛti (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6): Yājñavalkya's opening chapter [p-eb8c05, p-7d795b] defines dharma succinctly as 'cosmic order, sacred law, duty, righteousness, and prescribed conduct.' Its sources are 'Revelation (the Vedas), tradition (the Smṛtis), the conduct of the virtuous, what is pleasing to one's own self, and desire born of right resolve' (Yj_1.7 [p-7d795b]) — closely paralleling Manu but adding 'desire born of right resolve' as a fifth root. Yj_1.3 [p-b83e2e] extends the epistemic sources to fourteen, including Purāṇas, Nyāya, and Mīmāṃsā alongside the Vedas. Crucially, Yj_1.8 [p-1e45c4] identifies the 'vision of the Self through yoga' as the highest dharma above sacrifice, conduct, and giving — an interiorisation Manu does not foreground.

3. Parāśara Smṛti (unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be): Parāśara, addressing the Kali yuga specifically, presents dharma as age-relative: 'The dharmas for men are different in the Kṛta yuga, the Tretā, and the Dvāpara. They are different again in the Kali yuga, in accordance with the character of the age' (Par_1.22 [p-11656a]). He assigns charity (dāna) as the supreme dharma for Kali (Par_1.23 [p-afd7d9]), just as tapas was supreme in Kṛta. He adds a conduct-focused definition: 'Ācāra (proper conduct) is the protector of dharma for all four varṇas. For those whose bodies are corrupted by bad conduct, dharma turns away' (Par_1.37 [p-6c0070]), making right conduct not just an expression but an indispensable guardian of dharma.

4. Viṣṇu Smṛti (unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8): Viṣṇu begins (Vi_1.5 [p-8081a1]) by describing the Cosmic Boar as 'composed of dharma (sacred law) and truth' — dharma as the very substance of the divine. By chapter 2, dharma bifurcates into varna-specific duties (Vi_2.4-8 [p-b44991 ff.]: teaching for Brahmanas, arms for Kṣatriyas, cattle for Vaiśyas, service for Śūdras) and a universal common dharma of forbearance, truthfulness, self-control, purity, non-violence, and freedom from greed (Vi_2.16-17 [p-515a60, p-00e45d]).

5. Kātyāyana Smṛti (unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2) treats dharma primarily as operative legal category in judicial proceedings: dharma (sacred law) governs when facts are admitted and the rightful owner recovers property (K_035 [p-e58a0a]); it stands above custom and royal edict, though vyavahāra (legal procedure) may supersede it when reasoning displaces divine proof (K_039 [p-6e658f]). Kātyāyana also names kuladharma — family law transmitted through lineage — as a distinct sub-species that must be upheld (K_085 [p-2e0820]).

Synthesis: Across these five smriti works, 'dharma' operates simultaneously as (a) cosmic principle / metaphysical order separating right from wrong at creation; (b) revealed sacred law encoded in Veda and Smṛti; (c) socially particularised duty differentiated by varna, life-stage (āśrama), family (kuladharma), and cosmic age (yugadharma); (d) internalised virtue — what the heart of the good assents to; and (e) the highest form pointing toward Self-realisation (yoga, per Yājñavalkya). The texts converge on Veda + Smṛti + conduct of the virtuous + self-satisfaction as dharma's four authoritative roots, while diverging on its summit: tapas (Manu/Parāśara in Kṛta yuga), giving (Parāśara in Kali), or yogic self-knowledge (Yājñavalkya).
